items.title: Woman accused of flogging 12-year-old boy to death surrenders to Anambra police
items.description: By Vincent Ujumadu A 47-year-old woman, Mrs. Dim Ebere, who was accused of flogging a 12-year-old pupil to death in Adazi-Ani, Anaocha Local Government Area of Anambra State, has surrendered to the police. Ebere reportedly turned herself in at the Neni Police Station in the local government area following a sustained manhunt by operatives of the Anambra State Police Command’s Safe School Protection Squad and officers of the Neni Divisional Police Headquarters. The Police Public Relations Officer (PPRO) in Anambra State, SP Tochukwu Ikenga, confirmed the development, saying the suspect voluntarily presented herself and is currently in police custody. Ikenga said Ebere would be transferred to the State Criminal Investigation Department (SCID), Awka, where further investigation into the incident is ongoing. He commended residents of Adazi-Ani community, traditional leaders and other stakeholders for their cooperation and support, noting that their efforts contributed to the suspect’s decision to surrender. The police spokesperson assured the family of the deceased and members of the public that the command would ensure a thorough investigation and that justice would be served. He also appealed for calm, urging residents not to take the law into their own hands but to continue providing relevant information that could assist investigators. The incident was reported last week after police operatives responded to a distress call and rushed the victim to a hospital, where he was confirmed dead by a medical doctor. The remains of the deceased have been deposited in a hospital morgue pending an autopsy to determine further details surrounding the death. The post Woman accused of flogging 12-year-old boy to death surrenders to Anambra police appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: AliExpress frappé d’une amende européenne record de 629 millions de dollars
items.description: AliExpress, plateforme de commerce en ligne du groupe chinois Alibaba, a ete condamnee le 20 juillet par l'Union europeenne a une amende de 550 millions d'euros, soit environ 629 millions de dollars, pour des manquements dans la lutte contre la vente de produits illegaux, dangereux et contrefaits. Il s'agit de la troisieme sanction prononcee par la Commission europeenne dans le cadre du reglement sur les services numeriques (Digital Services Act, DSA), qui impose aux tres grandes plateformes en ligne de mieux prevenir la diffusion de contenus et de produits interdits. La Commission reproche a AliExpress de ne pas avoir correctement evalue les risques lies a la presence de marchandises illegales sur son site et de ne pas avoir mis en place des mesures suffisamment efficaces pour les limiter. Selon Bruxelles, des produits allant des articles contrefaits aux jouets dangereux ou aux cosmetiques presentant des risques seraient restes disponibles pendant plusieurs semaines sur la plateforme. Les autorites europeennes critiquent egalement les systemes de recommandation et de publicite d'AliExpress, estimant qu'ils ont contribue a amplifier la visibilite de produits interdits. La Commission pointe aussi du doigt le systeme d'autorisation des marques mis en place par AliExpress pour lutter contre les contrefacons. Selon elle, ce dispositif serait insuffisamment dote en personnel et facilement contournable par certains vendeurs. Une amende superieure a celles infligees a X et Temu La sanction intervient alors que les plateformes chinoises de commerce en ligne font l'objet d'une surveillance accrue en Europe. La commissaire europeenne chargee du numerique, Henna Virkkunen, a souligne qu'AliExpress comptait 193 millions d'utilisateurs europeens l'an dernier, contre 156 millions pour Shein et 130 millions pour Temu . « C'est tres dangereux pour les consommateurs et injuste pour les entreprises qui respectent nos regles », a-t-elle declare. Le montant de l'amende depasse largement les sanctions deja infligees dans le cadre du DSA, notamment les 120 millions d'euros imposes a X et les 200 millions d'euros infliges a Temu. AliExpress a rejete la sanction, estimant qu'elle etait « disproportionnee » et ne refletait pas les ameliorations engagees par l'entreprise. La plateforme a indique examiner la decision de Bruxelles et envisager « toutes les options disponibles ».

items.title: Humanity’s greatest challenge is leadership formation, not politics or technology — Ozigbo
items.description: By Chimaobi Nwaiwu NNEWI — Former Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Transcorp Group and 2021 Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) governorship candidate in Anambra State, Mr. Valentine Ozigbo, has said the greatest challenge facing humanity is not politics, economics or technology, but the quality of individuals being formed to lead society. Ozigbo stated this while delivering a keynote address titled, “The Making of a Transformational Leader: Service Above Self, Creating Lasting Impact Through Leadership,” at the installation ceremony of the 25th Silver Jubilee President of Rotary Club of Onitsha Metropolis, Hon. Dr. Smart Chinedu Ezuma, held at Soprom Hotel & Suites, Federal Housing Estate, Trans Nkisi, 3-3, Onitsha, Anambra State. The former Transcorp Plc boss said societies ultimately reflect the quality of people they produce, stressing that the strength of a nation depends largely on the quality of its leadership and the values of those who emerge as leaders. According to him, the world currently faces a crisis of character and formation despite remarkable advances in knowledge and technology. “We are living in one of the most extraordinary periods in human history. Never before have human beings possessed so much information, yet wisdom appears increasingly scarce. We have more universities than ever before, yet fewer people we genuinely trust. We have more technology, yet less peace; more wealth, yet less commitment; more communication, yet less understanding; and more leaders, yet fewer elders,” he said. Ozigbo argued that the fundamental question before every generation is not whether leaders can be produced, but the kind of leaders society is creating. He explained that true leadership begins before political office, appointment or public recognition, noting that transformational leaders must first undergo personal transformation. “Leadership begins long before position, but with formation. Before God entrusts a person with influence, He first shapes the person. People do not become transformational leaders merely because they receive titles. They become transformational leaders because they themselves have first been transformed,” he said. The business leader identified three stages of leadership development: leadership of self, leadership of others and leadership for transformation. He also spoke on the role of self-interest in human development, saying personal ambition is not inherently negative but must be guided by a broader sense of responsibility to society. According to him, self-interest can either contribute to societal progress or become destructive when individuals focus only on personal comfort, family, business interests and ambitions while ignoring the common good. He warned that citizens who detach themselves from societal challenges risk contributing to the decline of the communities they belong to. “No one succeeds alone. No family prospers permanently inside a collapsing society. No business flourishes indefinitely in a broken nation, and no amount of private success can compensate for public failure,” he said. Ezuma Unveils N28m Rotary Service Agenda In his installation address, titled “Service Above Self: Creating Lasting Impact Through Leadership,” the newly installed Rotary Club President, Hon. Dr. Smart Chinedu Ezuma, expressed gratitude to Bishop Dr. E.C. Onuorah, whom he credited with instilling in him values of compassion, humility, integrity and selfless service. Ezuma said his journey into Rotary reflected values he had embraced throughout his life, adding that the organisation provided a platform to apply those principles in service to humanity. He announced a N28 million intervention programme for his tenure as president, focusing on maternal and child healthcare, membership expansion, education and literacy, community economic development, disease prevention, vocational service, peace-building, water and sanitation, environmental initiatives and youth development. The installation ceremony attracted members of the Rotary community, dignitaries and other stakeholders who gathered to celebrate the new leadership of the club. The post Humanity’s greatest challenge is leadership formation, not politics or technology — Ozigbo appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: États-Unis : Meta poursuivi au Tennessee pour l’addiction des jeunes à Instagram
items.description: Le geant americain des reseaux sociaux Meta Platforms fait face a un nouveau proces aux etats-Unis. Le 20 juillet, un tribunal du Tennessee ouvre une procedure visant a determiner si Instagram a ete concu de maniere a encourager une utilisation addictive chez les adolescents, contribuant ainsi a une crise de sante mentale chez les jeunes. Le procureur general du Tennessee, Jonathan Skrmetti, accuse Meta d'avoir viole la legislation de protection des consommateurs de l'etat en developpant un produit qui pousserait les adolescents a une utilisation excessive, tout en minimisant publiquement les risques lies a sa plateforme. Selon la plainte, l'entreprise aurait omis de reveler des recherches internes montrant des effets negatifs d'Instagram sur certains jeunes utilisateurs. Les autorites affirment egalement que Mark Zuckerberg et plusieurs responsables de Meta auraient ete alertes par des employes sur ces risques, sans engager suffisamment d'actions pour les reduire. Notifications, Reels et lecture automatique dans le viseur Le dossier porte notamment sur plusieurs fonctionnalites d'Instagram jugees problematiques par l'etat du Tennessee : la lecture automatique des contenus, les videos courtes Reels, les notifications repetees ou encore les mecanismes qui encouragent les utilisateurs a rester connectes. Les autorites demandent au tribunal d'imposer des sanctions financieres et d'obliger Meta a modifier certaines fonctionnalites de la plateforme. La loi de protection des consommateurs du Tennessee prevoit des amendes pouvant atteindre 1 000 dollars par infraction. De son cote, Meta rejette ces accusations. L'entreprise affirme avoir developpe depuis plusieurs annees des outils de protection destines aux adolescents, notamment des parametres adaptes a leur âge et des controles permettant aux parents de mieux encadrer leur usage. Le groupe soutient egalement que les accusations portent essentiellement sur les contenus publies par les utilisateurs et invoque la protection offerte par la section 230 de la loi americaine sur les communications en ligne, qui limite la responsabilite des plateformes concernant les contenus de tiers. Meta dans le collimateur de la justice Le proces du Tennessee s'inscrit dans une vaste offensive judiciaire contre les grandes plateformes numeriques. Pratiquement tous les etats americains ont engage des procedures concernant l'impact presume des reseaux sociaux sur la sante mentale des enfants. Le proces, prevu pour durer environ sept semaines, intervient alors que Meta doit egalement affronter d'autres actions en justice en Californie. Une audience federale impliquant plusieurs etats est notamment prevue en août, concernant des accusations liees a la protection des donnees des enfants. Cette procedure est la deuxieme affaire portee par un etat americain contre Meta a atteindre le stade du proces. Plus tot cette annee, le Nouveau-Mexique avait obtenu une decision favorable d'un jury, qui avait estime que l'entreprise avait trompe les consommateurs sur la securite de Facebook, Instagram et WhatsApp. Le jury avait accorde 375 millions de dollars de dommages et interets a l'etat. Un juge examine encore d'eventuelles mesures supplementaires visant a contraindre Meta a modifier certaines pratiques.

items.title: The Event thinking mindset: How reflexive responses are slowing Ghana’s National development
items.description: A Nation of Responses, Not Systems There is a certain mindset that pervades our national life in Ghana. I call it the event thinking mindset. It is the habit of perceiving national challenges as discrete, isolated occurrences of events to be responded to, rather than as symptoms of underlying systems that must be understood, redesigned, and patiently maintained. It stands in direct opposition to what scholars of organisational and public policy call systems thinking. This is the discipline of seeing wholes, patterns, interconnections, and feedback loops rather than snapshots. The event thinker asks, what happened, and what shall we do about it? The systems thinker asks, what structures, incentives, and mental models keep producing this outcome, and how do we change them? The difference sounds academic, but it is not. It is the difference between a nation that floods every June and mourns every June, and a nation that drains, zones, and builds so that June becomes an ordinary month. It is the difference between commissioning a hospital and sustaining a health system. It is, I contend, one of the deepest psychological obstacles to Ghana’s development. Something we ignore and often don’t pay attention to. Our elders captured the danger long ago that we do not merely pour water on a fire without thinking of its aftermath. It warns against the reflex act that quenches the flame but ignores the cause of the burning. Yet as a nation, we have made the reflex our method of governance; almost as a ritual. As a psychologist, I recognise event thinking as more than a policy failure. It is a cognitive style; it is a way of framing reality that has become institutionalised in our public life. It has several identifiable features: First, it is reactive rather than anticipatory. The event thinker waits for the crisis to announce itself. Nothing is a problem until it becomes a spectacle. The gutters may choke for twelve months, but sanitation becomes a national concern only when the floodwaters carry away lives and livelihoods, and the cameras arrive. Second, it is episodic rather than continuous. Attention rises with the event and collapses with the news cycle. We declare days of mourning, general cleaning, constitute committees, hold press conferences. However, after these, the file closes until the next occurrence. Psychologists would recognise here the dynamics of availability bias, that is, what is vivid and recent dominates judgement, while the slow, unglamorous work of maintenance disappears from consciousness. Third, it is symbolic rather than structural. The event mindset craves visible gestures such as the commissioning ceremony, the sod-cutting, the launch, the taskforce. The reason is that gestures photograph well and signal responsiveness. Structures, by contrast, are invisible when they work. No one cuts a ribbon for a drainage master plan that quietly prevents a flood. Fourth, it is personalised rather than institutional. When the event occurs, we search for a culprit; a negligent officer, a wicked contractor, a failed minister rather than interrogating the system that made the failure probable. Blame is an event-sized explanation for a system-sized problem. Fifth, and most tragically, it has no memory. Event thinking treats each crisis as unprecedented. The 2015 June 3rd disaster, the recurring cholera outbreaks, the perennial collapse of school infrastructure- each arrives as though it were a surprise, because the mindset that governs our response does not accumulate learning. Systems learn; events merely recur. Manifestation One: Sanitation- The Eternal Clean-Up Exercise Nowhere is event thinking more visible than in our approach to sanitation. Ghana’s sanitation response is essentially ceremonial and episodic. We organise National Sanitation Day exercises, communal clean-ups, and periodic desilting campaigns, usually after a flood, an outbreak, or a presidential directive. These are events. They generate photographs of officials in Wellington boots, and they generate a fleeting sense of collective virtue. But sanitation is not an event. It is a system comprising waste generation patterns, collection logistics, landfill and treatment infrastructure, drainage engineering, land-use enforcement, behavioural norms, financing models, and institutional accountability. A clean-up exercise that is not embedded in a functioning collection and disposal system is, quite literally, moving refuse from one place to another. The gutter desilted in July chokes again by August because the system that fills it such as indiscriminate dumping, absent bins, unenforced by-laws, buildings on waterways, remains untouched. Now let us consider Accra’s flooding. Every rainy season, the same communities submerge. Every season, we respond with relief items, condolences, and promises. The event mindset frames the flood as a natural disaster, an act of God or of rain. The systems mindset recognises it as a designed outcome; the predictable product of building on Ramsar wetlands, of a drainage network engineered for a city one-third the current size, of permits issued for structures in waterways, and of enforcement institutions with neither teeth nor will. The rain is an event; the flood is a system. Until we grasp that distinction, we will keep drowning on schedule. It is not taboo to return for what you have forgotten. Sankofa is, at its heart, a systems principle: it insists that the past must inform the present, that experience must accumulate into wisdom. Event thinking is the anti-Sankofa. It forgets deliberately, season after season. Manifestation Two: Health Infrastructure- Commissioning Buildings, Not Building Systems Our health sector reveals a second face of the event mindset. This is the confusion of infrastructure with systems. Across the Fourth Republic we have measured health progress in buildings including hospitals promised, sod cut, facilities commissioned. The building is the event; it is visible, countable, ribbon-ready. However, a hospital building is not healthcare. Healthcare is a system of trained personnel who stay, supply chains that deliver, equipment that is maintained and serviced, referral pathways that function, emergency transport that arrives, health financing that does not collapse under arrears, and community-level prevention that reduces the burden before it reaches the facility gate. Across the country, one finds completed or half-completed health facilities standing without staff, without equipment, or without operational budgets, the monuments that reflect the event mindset. The commissioning happened; the system never did. The same reflex governs our response to health crises. An outbreak occurs; we mobilise impressively just we did during the COVID-19 pandemic. However, between outbreaks, surveillance systems are starved, public health budgets thin out, and preventive infrastructure decays. We fund the war and neglect the army. Mental health offers a particularly painful example from my own field: attention surges when a tragedy makes headlines. For instance, when a suicide, or a violent incident attributed to psychiatric illness occurs, the nation briefly discovers that its psychiatric hospitals are congested and its Mental Health Authority underfunded. The event however, passes, and with it the attention. The Mental Health Act of 2012 gave us the architecture of a system; the event mindset has ensured that, more than a decade on, key provisions such as a sustainable funding levy remain substantially unimplemented. The law was the event; the system was never resourced. This is a very sad reality. Manifestation Three: Education- Reform as Spectacle Education displays a third face of the mindset: reform as recurring spectacle. Ghana has changed its educational structure repeatedly across the decades. We have been adjusting durations, renaming levels, introducing and withdrawing programmes often with each political transition. Each reform arrives as an event, announced with fanfare, branded, and politically owned. What rarely follows is the systemic work that makes any reform succeed: teacher preparation and motivation, learning materials that arrive on time, infrastructure that keeps pace with enrolment, assessment systems that measure real learning, and, crucially, the patience to let a reform mature across more than one electoral cycle. Free Senior High School illustrates the pattern with painful clarity. As a statement of equity, it is noble; access matters profoundly. The policy was however executed as an event; an announcement, while the system it depended upon (e.g., classrooms, dormitories, food supply chains, teacher numbers) lagged behind, producing the double-track improvisation and chronic feeding crises. The event was celebrated; the system groaned. Meanwhile, the deeper systemic questions such what are children actually learning? Why do many pupils complete basic school without functional literacy? — receive no ceremony, and therefore little attention. Learning is a system outcome; it cannot be commissioned. There is also the infrastructure ritual we all know: a school collapses, or children are photographed studying under trees, the image goes viral, and a response is mobilised for that school. The event is resolved; the system of school infrastructure planning, maintenance budgeting, and equitable distribution continues undisturbed, quietly preparing the next viral photograph. Why the Event Mindset Persists: A Psychological and Cultural Reflection If event thinking is so costly, why does it endure? Several forces sustain it. Political incentives reward events. In a four-year electoral cycle, visible events such as buildings, launches, distributions are electorally legible. Systems work is slow, invisible, and its benefits often ripen under a successor’s administration. No politician wins votes for a sewer. The rational politician therefore produces events; the mindset is, in part, an incentive structure wearing a cognitive disguise. Crisis response is psychologically satisfying. There is a documented human pull toward the drama of rescue over the tedium of prevention. The firefighter is a hero; the building inspector who prevented the fire is invisible. Our public emotions and our media are calibrated to events. Scarcity narrows the cognitive horizon. Research on the psychology of scarcity shows that persistent resource pressure tunnels attention toward the immediate and urgent, taxing the bandwidth needed for long-range planning. A state perpetually managing fiscal emergencies governs in event mode almost by necessity, and so scarcity and event thinking feed each other in a vicious loop. A theology of intervention can be misread into governance. As a pastor, I say this with care and from within the household of faith. I see that there is a strand of popular religiosity that frames life primarily as a sequence of divine events: breakthroughs, deliverances, miracles, rather than as stewardship of processes. Genesis 2:15 tells us the Lord placed man in the garden “to dress it and to keep it” (KJV). The Hebrew verbs are instructive: “ʿāḇaḏ” (to work, to serve) and “šāmar” (to keep, to guard, to maintain). This is systems language: cultivation and maintenance, not event language. A faith that prays for the miracle harvest but neglects the discipline of the garden has misread its own Scriptures. God ordained seedtime and harvest (Genesis 8:22), a system, with seasons, sequence, and patient process. The miracle-only mentality, transposed into civic life, becomes the expectation that development will arrive as an event: an oil discovery, a donor windfall, a messianic leader, rather than be built as a system. Event thinking reproduces itself because it works briefly. The clean-up does clear the gutter for a while. The relief items do feed the flood victims. The emergency recruitment does staff the hospital for a season. Each partial success rewards the reflex and postpones the reckoning. Towards a Systems Mindset: What Must Change Naming the pathology is easier than curing it, but the direction of cure is clear. We must learn to see structures beneath events. Every recurring national problem should trigger a disciplined question: what keeps producing this? Recurrence is diagnostic. A problem that returns annually is not an event; it is a system announcing itself. Our public institutions need the analytical habit, and the honesty, to map the feedback loops: how unenforced by-laws feed flooding, how unmotivated teachers feed learning poverty, how unmaintained equipment feeds hospital deaths. We must institutionalise memory. Systems learn through documentation, evaluation, and continuity. Committee reports must become living instruments with tracked implementation, not archived condolences. National development planning must be insulated, as far as constitutionally possible, from the four-year amnesia of political transition. Sankofa must become administrative practice, not merely an adinkra symbol on our fabrics. We must celebrate maintenance. A cultural revaluation is needed in which the engineer who keeps the drains flowing, the biomedical technician who keeps the theatre lights on, and the headteacher who keeps a school functioning for twenty years are honoured above the ribbon-cutters. What a society celebrates, it reproduces. Our children are currently absorbing the lesson that launching matters more than sustaining. We must teach them otherwise, by what we honour. We must redesign incentives. Budgeting should privilege recurrent and maintenance expenditure, not only capital projects. Performance frameworks for public officials should measure system outcomes; learning levels, disease burden reduction, drainage functionality, and not activity counts. Civil society and the media must mature from event coverage to systems accountability. The church and other moral institutions must preach process. Those of us who shape the nation’s moral imagination from the pulpit bear a particular duty. We have to recover a theology of stewardship, diligence, and faithful process; the Joseph who planned fourteen years ahead (Genesis 41), not only the Israel that cried out in crisis. Faith and planning are not rivals; Proverbs 21:31 holds them together: “The horse is prepared against the day of battle: but safety is of the LORD” (KJV). Preparation is ours; we cannot pray away what we have refused to plan against. Concluding thoughts: From the Reflex to the Design Ghana is not short of intelligence, energy, or compassion. Our event responses prove all three: we mobilise magnificently when the waters rise. What we lack is the collective cognitive discipline to stay engaged when the waters recede; to convert crisis energy into design energy, mourning into mapping, reflex into architecture. The event thinking mindset is comfortable because it demands only reaction. The systems mindset is costly because it demands foresight, patience, memory, and the humility to work invisibly. But nations are not built by events. They are built by systems; maintained daily, improved incrementally, and handed on faithfully. Nations are not products of accidents; they rather develop from intentionality. Until we make that psychological migration, from the reflex to the design, our development will remain what it has too long been: a series of impressive responses to entirely predictable disasters. When you pluck one stick from the broom, it breaks; bound together, the broom does not break. The proverb is usually preached on unity. But it is equally a parable of systems: strength lies not in the single dramatic stick, but in the binding; the unglamorous, deliberate act of holding many parts together so that they function as one. That binding is systems thinking. It is time Ghana learned to sweep with the whole broom. ©️ 2026 Prof Joseph Osafo Adu

items.title: ONU : Le Sénégal apporte son soutien officiel à la candidature de Macky Sall
items.description: Le président de la République, Bassirou Diomaye Diakhar Faye, a décidé d'apporter le plein soutien du Sénégal à la candidature de son prédécesseur, Macky Sall, au poste de Secrétaire général de l'Organisation des Nations Unies (ONU), à l'issue d'une audience accordée à l'ancien chef de l'État au Palais de la République. Selon un communiqué du ministère de l'Intégration africaine, des Affaires étrangères et des Sénégalais de l'extérieur, Macky Sall a été reçu le vendredi 17 juillet 2026 afin de présenter au président de la République sa candidature à la tête de l'ONU. Le document précise que la rencontre s'est déroulée « dans un climat empreint de cordialité et de courtoisie républicaine », illustrant l'attachement du chef de l'État « au dialogue, à la continuité de l'État et au rassemblement autour des intérêts supérieurs de la Nation ». À l'issue de cet entretien, Bassirou Diomaye Faye a décidé que le Sénégal soutiendra officiellement la candidature de Macky Sall. Le chef de l'État a, dans cette perspective, instruit le Gouvernement ainsi que l'ensemble du réseau diplomatique sénégalais de se mobiliser pour promouvoir cette candidature auprès des États membres des Nations Unies. Le président de la République a également lancé un appel aux forces vives de la Nation afin qu'elles soutiennent cette candidature, désormais présentée comme celle du Sénégal, « au service de l'Afrique et d'un multilatéralisme plus performant », conclut le communiqué. Salla GUEYE

items.title: Italie : la mort d’un Marocain lors de son interpellation par la police provoque une vague de protestations
items.description: La mort d’Abderrahim Faqir, un ressortissant marocain de 43 ans, lors de son interpellation par la police à Bologne, dans le nord de l’Italie, a déclenché une vague d’indignation et de protestations. Le parquet de Bologne a ouvert une enquête afin de déterminer les circonstances exactes du décès, tandis que la famille, des associations et plusieurs organisations réclament une enquête indépendante et transparente. Les faits se sont produits dimanche soir dans la rue Svevo, dans le quartier Pilastro. Selon les premiers éléments communiqués par les autorités italiennes, la police était intervenue après avoir été alertée de la présence d’un homme décrit comme étant dans un état d’agitation. Les policiers auraient utilisé du gaz au poivre avant de maîtriser l’homme au sol et de le menotter. Celui-ci aurait ensuite été victime d’un malaise et serait décédé malgré l’intervention des secours. Une vidéo montrant les dernières minutes de l’intervention a toutefois suscité une vive émotion. Sur les images diffusées sur les réseaux sociaux, le ressortissant marocain apparaît immobilisé au sol par plusieurs policiers et répète : « Je n’arrive pas à respirer… Aidez-moi ». La séquence a rapidement soulevé des interrogations sur les conditions de son interpellation et la proportionnalité de la force utilisée. Le parquet de Bologne a ouvert une enquête pour faire toute la lumière sur le décès. Les policiers ayant participé à l’intervention pourraient être inscrits au registre des personnes faisant l’objet d’une enquête, une procédure permettant notamment aux autorités judiciaires de mener les investigations nécessaires. Les enregistrements des caméras-piétons portées par les agents devraient également être examinés, tout comme les témoignages disponibles et les résultats des examens médicaux destinés à déterminer précisément les causes de la mort. Selon des médias italiens, Abderrahim Faqir vivait depuis plusieurs années en Italie et dirigeait une petite entreprise spécialisée dans les services de chargement et de déchargement. Sa sœur a accusé les policiers d’avoir provoqué sa mort et réclame que toute la vérité soit faite sur les circonstances du drame. L’affaire a rapidement dépassé le cadre judiciaire pour provoquer une mobilisation à Bologne et dans d’autres villes italiennes. Des rassemblements et des manifestations ont été organisés afin de réclamer justice et davantage de transparence sur les circonstances du décès. Le syndicat italien Sindacato Intercategoriale Cobas a également annoncé une grève de 24 heures en réaction à l’affaire. Le président de la région Émilie-Romagne, Michele de Pascale, et le maire de Bologne, Matteo Lepore, ont présenté leurs condoléances à la famille du défunt et appelé à faire toute la lumière sur les faits avec rapidité, précision et transparence. La communauté marocaine en Italie suit également l’affaire avec une grande attention. Yassine Belkassem, coordinateur national du Réseau des associations de la communauté marocaine en Italie (RACMI), a réclamé une enquête urgente et transparente permettant d’établir les responsabilités éventuelles. L’affaire suscite également des réactions politiques et associatives en Italie. Plusieurs voix de l’opposition et organisations de défense des droits humains demandent que les méthodes d’intervention policière dans ce type de situation soient examinées. Les autorités italiennes soulignent, de leur côté, que l’enquête devra établir précisément le déroulement des faits à partir des vidéos, des témoignages, des caméras portées par les policiers et des conclusions médico-légales. Ces investigations seront déterminantes pour établir les causes exactes du décès et déterminer si des responsabilités doivent être engagées. The post Italie : la mort d’un Marocain lors de son interpellation par la police provoque une vague de protestations appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .

items.title: Xi Jinping appelle à une gouvernance mondiale « juste et équitable » de l’intelligence artificielle
items.description: Le président chinois Xi Jinping a appelé vendredi la communauté internationale à promouvoir une intelligence artificielle (IA) centrée sur l'être humain, orientée vers le progrès et le bien commun, afin d'en faire « une grande force motrice pour la prospérité partagée et la sécurité commune ». S'exprimant lors de la cérémonie d'ouverture de la Conférence mondiale sur l'intelligence artificielle (WAIC) 2026 et de la Réunion de haut niveau sur la gouvernance mondiale en matière d'IA, organisées à Shanghai, dans l'est de la Chine, le dirigeant chinois a plaidé pour la mise en place d'un système de gouvernance mondiale de l'IA « juste et équitable ». Dans son discours, Xi Jinping a souligné que le développement rapide de l'intelligence artificielle soulève des défis majeurs pour l'humanité. Il a notamment évoqué les questions liées à la coexistence entre l'homme et des machines de plus en plus capables de raisonner, à la sécurité lorsque les algorithmes participent à la prise de décision, à l'adaptation de la gouvernance face à des technologies qui mettent à l'épreuve les principes éthiques, ainsi qu'à la nécessité de garantir un partage équitable des bénéfices de l'IA alors que les écarts technologiques continuent de se creuser. Le président chinois a affirmé que tous les pays devaient adopter une approche centrée sur l'être humain dans le développement de l'intelligence artificielle, afin que cette technologie serve le progrès de l'humanité. Il a appelé à renforcer la coopération internationale et à bâtir ensemble un cadre mondial de gouvernance de l'IA fondé sur l'équité, l'inclusivité et le bénéfice partagé. Selon Xi Jinping, une telle démarche permettra de faire de l'intelligence artificielle un moteur de développement commun, tout en contribuant au renforcement de la sécurité collective à l'échelle mondiale.

items.title: Un soldat russe vit en moyenne 20 à 30 minutes sur le front ukrainien
items.description: Mercredi dernier, le 15 juillet, le directeur de la CIA, John Ratcliffe, a déclaré que les renseignements américains confirmaient des informations en sources ouvertes, selon lesquelles l’espérance de vie moyenne d’une recrue russe arrivant actuellement sur le front en Ukraine « est estimée entre 20 et 30 minutes » CIA Says AI Drones Give Russian Troops Only 30 Minutes to Live », Bloomberg , 15 juillet 2026.'> 1 . En novembre, le secrétaire d’État américain, Marco Rubio, avait déclaré que 1 000 soldats russes étaient tués chaque jour Secretary of State Marco Rubio Remarks to the Press , Département d'État américain, 12 novembre 2025.'> 2 . Selon Ratcliffe, l’espérance de vie très courte des soldats russes déployés en Ukraine est la conséquence directe de l’amélioration des capacités des drones. Grâce à de nouveaux programmes d’intelligence artificielle et à des avancées techniques, ces derniers sont devenus « de véritables machines à tuer, à la fois spécialisées et peu coûteuses ». Au premier semestre, les pertes russes étaient 8 fois plus importantes que les pertes ukrainiennes, contre 2 à 3 fois plus en moyenne sur la période 2022-2025 Russian Blood and Treasure. The Ballooning Costs of Putin’s War , CSIS, 1er juillet 2026.'> 3 . Alors que le nombre de soldats russes mis hors de combat a atteint les 18 et 19 juillet son niveau le plus élevé (plus de 1 350 tués et blessés chaque jour) depuis le lancement de l’invasion à grande échelle, Moscou fait face à des problèmes de recrutement qui pourraient avoir un impact sur ses capacités opérationnelles en Ukraine. C’est notamment le cas au sein des Forces de systèmes sans pilote, la branche de l’armée dédiée aux drones. Seulement 8 000 opérateurs ont été formés au cours du premier semestre – 4 fois moins qu’initialement prévu à cette date (34 400), selon des documents officiels obtenus par le média indépendant russe iStories В беспилотные войска хотят набрать 80 тыс. контрактников до конца года », Важные истории , 5 mars 2026.'> 4 . Selon l’économiste Janis Kluge, seulement 71 200 nouveaux combattants au total auraient été recrutés dans les rangs de l’armée russe au premier trimestre, soit le chiffre le plus faible depuis trois ans. Ces difficultés suscitent de nombreuses rumeurs en Russie quant à une nouvelle mobilisation, qui pourrait être décrétée par le Kremlin après les élections législatives de septembre. Une seule vague de mobilisation a eu lieu à ce jour depuis le début de la guerre, en septembre 2022. Depuis plusieurs mois, de nombreux Russes astreints aux obligations militaires ont reçu, via le portail public Gosuslugi ( Госуслуги ), des convocations pour « mettre à jour » leurs informations personnelles dans les bureaux d'enregistrement de l’armée « Риск перестал быть теоретическим ». Ждать ли новой мобилизации в России ? », Радио Свобода , 14 juillet 2026.'> 5 . Après la mobilisation de 2022, qui avait révélé d’importantes défaillances en matière d'organisation, ainsi que des informations obsolètes, Moscou a lancé la création d’un système d’enregistrement numérique et d’un registre électronique, opérationnel depuis l'été 2025. Il doit notamment permettre aux autorités de croiser les données administratives pour appliquer des restrictions – interdiction de sortie du territoire, blocage de démarches immobilières ou administratives – aux appelés qui ne se présenteraient pas. L’article Un soldat russe vit en moyenne 20 à 30 minutes sur le front ukrainien est apparu en premier sur Le Grand Continent .

items.title: Coopération parlementaire: l’UIP réaffirme son soutien au Burkina
items.description: En visite de travail et de solidarité au Burkina Faso, la présidente de l'Union interparlementaire (UIP), Dr Tulia Ackson, a été reçue, ce lundi 20 juillet 2026 à Ouagadougou, par le président de l'Assemblée législative du peuple (ALP), Dr Ousmane Bougouma. Les échanges ont porté sur le renforcement de la coopération parlementaire, les défis sécuritaires et les perspectives d'accompagnement de l'institution burkinabè. Le président de l'Assemblée législative du peuple (ALP), Dr Ousmane Bougouma, a reçu en audience, ce lundi 20 juillet 2026 à Ouagadougou, la présidente de l'Union interparlementaire (UIP), Dr Tulia Ackson. Cette rencontre s'inscrit dans le cadre d'une visite de travail et de solidarité effectuée par la responsable de l'organisation parlementaire internationale au Burkina Faso du 19 au 25 juillet 2026. Les deux personnalités ont échangé sur les liens de coopération entre l'UIP et l'Assemblée législative du peuple, ainsi que sur les principaux défis auxquels le Burkina Faso est confronté, notamment sur les plans sécuritaire et humanitaire. À l'issue de l'entretien, Dr Tulia Ackson a expliqué que sa présence au Burkina Faso visait avant tout à témoigner de la solidarité de l'Union interparlementaire envers le peuple burkinabè, le Gouvernement et l'Assemblée législative du peuple. Elle a salué les efforts déployés par les autorités pour le rétablissement de la paix et de la stabilité, tout en mettant en avant la coopération entre le Burkina Faso, le Mali et le Niger dans la lutte contre le terrorisme. La présidente de l'UIP a également relevé les avancées institutionnelles enregistrées par le Burkina Faso, notamment la mise en place de l'Assemblée législative du peuple. Selon elle, cette évolution traduit la volonté des autorités de renforcer la gouvernance et de mieux répondre aux attentes des citoyens. Abordant les perspectives de coopération, Dr Tulia Ackson a réaffirmé l'engagement de l'Union interparlementaire à poursuivre son accompagnement au profit du Parlement burkinabè. Cet appui se traduira notamment par des programmes de renforcement des capacités destinés aux députés et au personnel administratif, portant sur les procédures législatives, le contrôle de l'action gouvernementale ainsi que les réponses parlementaires aux enjeux sécuritaires. À travers cette audience, l'Assemblée législative du peuple et l'Union interparlementaire réaffirment leur volonté de consolider un partenariat fondé sur la solidarité, le renforcement des capacités institutionnelles et la promotion d'une gouvernance parlementaire, capable de répondre efficacement aux attentes des populations et aux défis auxquels le Burkina Faso est confronté. La présidente de l'Union interparlementaire (UIP), Dr Tulia Ackson, a, également, rencontré les femmes parlementaires de l'Assemblée législative du peuple (ALP) pour un moment d'échanges placé sous le signe du dialogue, du partage d'expériences et de la promotion du leadership féminin. Ça a été l’occasion d’échanger sur le rôle des femmes dans les institutions parlementaires, de leur contribution à la consolidation de la démocratie ainsi que des défis liés à une participation accrue des femmes aux processus de décision. Elle a salué l'engagement des femmes parlementaires burkinabè, tout en les encourageant à poursuivre leurs efforts en faveur d'une gouvernance inclusive, au service des populations. Par Valentin SOMANDE L’article Coopération parlementaire: l'UIP réaffirme son soutien au Burkina est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: TikToker “Bawumia Ba” arrested again over alleged offensive conduct
items.description: The Ghana Police Service has re-arrested 26-year-old TikToker Mahama Aminat, popularly known as “Bawumia Ba,” over an alleged case of offensive conduct. The social media personality has been put before the Accra Circuit Court to answer the charge, while investigations into the matter continue. According to the Police, the re-arrest forms part of ongoing efforts to investigate allegations surrounding content attributed to the TikToker. She is expected to go through the legal process as authorities continue to gather further information. Mahama Aminat previously came into the public spotlight following her activities on TikTok, where she shared political commentary and other social media content, earning her the nickname “Bawumia Ba” due to her perceived support for former Vice President Dr. Mahamudu Bawumia. Her arrest has generated discussions on social media, with some users calling for due process while others await the outcome of the court proceedings. The Police Service has reiterated that investigations are ongoing and that the matter will be handled in accordance with the law. The Inspector-General of Police’s Cyber Vetting and Enforcement Team (CVET) first arrested Mahama Aminat in May 2026 after she was seen in videos circulating on TikTok making insulting comments about President John Mahama and allegedly issuing threats against his life and that of his wife, Lordina Mahama, while also encouraging others to engage in violent acts. She was subsequently arraigned before the Accra Circuit Court 2 and granted bail in the sum of GH¢1 million with two sureties. Under the bail conditions, one of the sureties must be a civil servant earning not less than GH¢5,000. She was also required to deposit her passport or Ghana Card with the police and report regularly to investigative authorities as part of her bail terms.

items.title: Guerre contre l’Iran : un risque électoral croissant pour Trump avant les élections de mi-mandat
items.description: a quatre mois des elections americaines de mi-mandat, la guerre contre l'Iran s'impose comme un enjeu politique majeur pour Donald Trump . L'effet de rassemblement espere par la Maison Blanche apres la reprise des attaques contre Teheran semble s'etre dissipe, laissant place a des inquietudes croissantes sur le coût economique et la duree du conflit. Alors que le president avait fait campagne sur la promesse de mettre fin aux « guerres sans fin », l'enlisement du dossier iranien fragilise desormais sa majorite et complique la strategie electorale republicaine. La reprise des hostilites le 8 juillet a replace la politique etrangere au cœur des preoccupations des electeurs, mais sous un angle essentiellement economique. Les Americains associent de plus en plus le conflit a la hausse des prix de l'energie, aux depenses militaires et aux risques d'inflation. Selon plusieurs enquetes d'opinion, une large majorite des citoyens s'attend desormais a un engagement militaire prolonge et seuls une minorite soutiennent la poursuite des attaques. Beaucoup estiment que les coûts financiers de la guerre depassent desormais ses benefices strategiques. Cette evolution pese sur la popularite du president, retombee autour de 34 %, tandis que la guerre accentue les fractures au sein du Parti republicain. Les partisans d'une ligne dure continuent de defendre une pression maximale sur Teheran, mais le courant « America First » se montre beaucoup plus reserve face a une intervention appelee a durer. Cette aile du parti accepte des operations limitees mais refuse un engagement militaire sans echeance claire, des depenses illimitees ou une strategie dictee par les interets d'autres partenaires regionaux. Une economie incertaine Le vice-president JD Vance tente d'incarner cette ligne intermediaire en soutenant les premieres frappes tout en rejetant une escalade permanente. Il cherche a preserver la coherence du discours isolationniste qui a largement contribue au succes electoral de Donald Trump aupres de son electorat. La disparition de Lindsey Graham modifie egalement les equilibres internes du camp republicain. Figure influente des questions de defense, il jouait un role de passerelle entre l'establishment republicain et Donald Trump sur les dossiers militaires. Son absence complique la recherche d'un consensus au moment ou les debats sur les credits de defense et le financement de la guerre s'intensifient au Congres. L'economie demeure toutefois le principal facteur de risque. Les tensions dans le detroit d'Ormuz ont rencheri le coût du petrole, provoquant une remontee du prix de l'essence et alimentant les craintes inflationnistes. Cette situation pourrait egalement contraindre la Reserve federale a maintenir des taux d'interet eleves plus longtemps, compliquant davantage la situation economique a quelques mois du scrutin.

items.title: NPP meetings in support of Wontumi won’t change court’s verdict – Awula Serwah
items.description: Convener of the Economic Conscious Citizens, Awula Serwah, says any meetings or support activities organised by the New Patriotic Party (NPP) for its Ashanti Regional Chairman, Bernard Antwi-Boasiako, popularly known as Chairman Wontumi, will not change the verdict of the court. Her comments follow the decision by the NPP leadership to commence a review of the court’s judgment in the case involving Mr Boasiako as the party considers its next steps following his conviction and 20-year prison sentence. Speaking on JoyNews’ The Pulse , Madam Serwah criticised efforts to politically defend individuals convicted over activities she believes have contributed to environmental destruction. “The NPP can have as many meetings as they want to have. If they want to back somebody who the court has found guilty of destroying our forest reserves and poisoning our water bodies, so be it,” she stated. According to her, illegal mining remains an existential threat to Ghana, and political considerations should not overshadow the need to protect the country’s natural resources. Madam Serwah added that the conviction should serve as a catalyst for further prosecutions of other individuals accused of engaging in illegal mining activities. She stressed that the fight against galamsey must focus on accountability and ensure that all persons found culpable, regardless of political affiliation, face the law. She further stated that Chairman Wontumi and his legal team have the right to pursue an appeal, as provided under Ghana’s legal system, but stressed that the court’s decision must be respected. “Our legal system allows for appeal, so Chairman Wontumi and his legal team are welcome to appeal at the Supreme Court where the decision would be final,” she said.

items.title: Ave Maria School to introduce AI and drone training in September
items.description: Ave Maria School Limited, a leading private basic education institution in the Greater Region, is set to roll out Artificial Intelligence (AI) and drone lessons as part of its commitment to technology and innovation, starting in September this year. The school will introduce training in AI and drone flying from next term to equip learners with skills for global success in the 21st century. The announcement was made by the head teacher of the school, Mr. Anthony Afful, last Saturday during the school’s annual graduation ceremony held on campus at Dansoman in Accra. The event was attended by parents, teachers, and members of the school management board, including the CEO of the school, Mr Michael Sarfo Antwi, and the Chairman, Dr. Mensah Bonsu. Speaking on the theme “Harnessing Skills for Global Success in the 21st Century”, Mr. Afful said the theme was timely given how modern ICT is driving economic growth globally. “As a school, we have therefore resolved to constantly integrate the required skills in modern technology into our mainstream curriculum of study,” he said. “From next term, as part of our existing Practical Skills Development Programme, we are introducing our very talented and critical-thinking learners to the basics of artificial intelligence (AI) and the flying of drones”. “He appealed to parents to support their children in enrolling in the new program and provide them with the needed resources. “It is our strongest belief that such a contemporary course of study will be of immense benefit to these children in the future,” Mr. Afful added. Academic Performance and BECE Preparations The head teacher also commended students for their improved performance in competitive mock exams throughout the year. “In all, the student groups sat competitive mocks and showed steady improvement in their performance, mock after mock, through robust academic activities including classes on Saturdays and Sundays,” he said. He expressed optimism that the 2026 Basic Education Certificate Examination results will be encouraging and provide a basis for replicating the same strategies for the next batch of BECE candidates writing in May 2026. Advice to Graduates and Parents Mr. Afful had special words for the graduating JHS students. He reminded them that completing JHS was “not an end in itself, but an important means to an end”. “Today’s occasion, therefore, marks one end of your journey and the beginning of yet another crucial journey. As you move into senior high school, campus life will offer you a lot of exposure, positive and negative, each of which has the potential to make or break your life,” he cautioned. He urged them to choose friends wisely and to stay away from negative influences. “Be proud of yourselves. Many people never even started school, and many who did did not finish. You started, and you have finished. Congratulations, my children,” he said. To parents and guardians, Mr. Afful acknowledged their sacrifices and urged them not to relent. “The work is still not finished. Continue, therefore, to invest in your children’s education and in God’s own good time. Your labour shall never be in vain, and you will reap maximum returns on your investment,” he said. He thanked parents, guests, staff, and students for attending the ceremony and wished everyone a memorable graduation. Ave Maria School currently runs the Oxford International Curriculum and has placed strong emphasis on academic rigour and practical skills development over the past year. Guest Speaker Commends School Speaking as Guest Speaker, Mr. Johnson Selorm Abayi, Curriculum Implementation and Training Manager for the Oxford International Curriculum in Ghana, said Ave Maria School stood out among Oxford-accredited schools across Ghana. “Out of all these schools, I put on record today that there is something truly special about Ave Maria School. It is one of the most organized schools currently implementing the Oxford International Curriculum in Ghana,” he said. “You have focused leadership. You have dedicated staff with an admirable spirit of learning.” Mr. Abayi noted that education in the 21st century must shift from teacher-centred, exam-driven to learner-centred. He said true excellence is measured not only by academic performance but also by critical thinking, creativity, resilience, collaboration, and problem-solving. “Parents should not become overly excited simply because a school says their child is the best in one academic subject. Neither should they become discouraged if their child struggles in one subject. The world is looking for individuals with the skills to identify problems and develop meaningful solutions,” he added. Call for Practical Skills Mr. Abayi urged Ave Maria School to fully leverage the Oxford International Curriculum’s focus areas, including Wellbeing, Sustainability, Global Skills, Computing, Technology and Artificial Intelligence. He said the goal should be for every learner to leave with more than a certificate. “Whether it is learning to play a musical instrument, singing, participating in sports, swimming, catering, public speaking, coding, robotics, entrepreneurship, or football, every learner should graduate with skills that prepare them for life beyond the classroom,” he said. He also called on the school to keep parents actively involved and to communicate clearly as new teaching approaches are introduced. Advice to Parents and Learners To parents, Mr. Abayi said the relationship with the school should be a partnership, not a customer-service arrangement. He urged them to avoid comparing children and to promote the use of home languages, which he described as key to identity and deeper learning. Addressing the graduates, he described them as “lights” and “problem-solvers” for Ghana and the world. He encouraged responsible use of technology. “These technologies are tools that can help you learn, create, innovate, and solve problems. Learn to use technology responsibly. Let technology become your servant, not your master,” he said. Mr. Abayi congratulated the school on completing its first academic year under the Oxford International Curriculum and reaffirmed Oxford University Press and Catalyst Learning Systems’ commitment to supporting the school. Over 46 junior high students graduated and were presented with certificates, while outstanding students received special awards. Ms. Jean Francess Chantal Akpali, who claimed 6 awards, was adjudged the overall best student. Apart from her awards, the school PTA presented her with an undisclosed cash prize.

items.title: Here’s the latest.
40 women, children feared dead as boat capsizes in Jigawa River
items.description: At least 40 women and children are feared dead after a canoe conveying passengers capsized in Ringim Local Government Area of Jigawa State. The Jigawa State Police Command confirmed the incident on Monday, stating that search and rescue operations were ongoing to locate the missing passengers. In a statement shared on its official Facebook page, the command disclosed that one body had been recovered, while efforts were underway to recover the remaining victims. “One of the bodies was recovered. Efforts to recover the 39 are underway,” the police said. The Director of Planning at the Jigawa State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA), Muhammad Sanusi, also confirmed the incident, saying the victims were mostly female farmers and agricultural workers who were travelling in a canoe when the accident occurred. Sanusi said the exact cause of the boat mishap had not been established, adding that emergency officials, local divers and community members were involved in the search for possible survivors and victims. He explained that authorities were still working to determine the actual number of people on board and the number of casualties recorded. “Hands are on deck as combined efforts continue to determine the exact number of those rescued or missing,” he said. The incident adds to concerns over recurring boat accidents in parts of northern Nigeria, where many rural communities depend on waterways as a major means of transportation. Such accidents have often been linked to factors including overloading, poor maintenance of vessels and inadequate use of safety equipment such as life jackets. Authorities are expected to provide further updates as rescue operations continue. The post 40 women, children feared dead as boat capsizes in Jigawa River appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Banking Keta for decades, yet investing too little in Keta
items.description: For more than half a century, GCB Bank has occupied a commanding position in the commercial and social life of Keta and the wider Anlo area. Public servants, pensioners, traders, professionals, traditional authorities, fisherfolk, artisans, entrepreneurs and established businesses have entrusted their salaries, savings and commercial transactions to the Bank. This long-standing relationship has given GCB a strong and profitable presence in the local economy. That history, however, raises a legitimate and unavoidable public-interest question: what visible, sustained and proportionate community investment has accompanied the commercial value that GCB Bank has derived from Keta and Anlo? Many residents and customers struggle to identify major and continuous corporate social responsibility interventions associated with the Keta branch. The issue is not that the Bank must finance every local programme, nor that all income earned locally must be spent locally. The issue is that a major national financial institution should demonstrate a deliberate, transparent and measurable commitment to the community that has sustained it for generations. From Commercial Presence to Shared Value Modern corporate citizenship goes beyond receiving deposits, granting loans and declaring profits. Businesses are increasingly judged by the extent to which they support local enterprise, protect the environment, improve customer experience and respond to the vulnerabilities of the communities in which they operate. A bank that benefits from public-sector salaries, market commerce, household savings and small-business activity should not remain socially invisible. The immediate frontage and surroundings of a major financial institution communicate its values. Where pedestrian access, drainage, sanitation, landscaping and the adjoining streets appear neglected, the public may reasonably question whether the institution identifies with the dignity and development of its host community. GCB Bank should work with the Keta Municipal Assembly, traditional authorities and market leadership to improve the environment around its branch through practical interventions such as drainage improvement, street lighting, waste receptacles, disability-friendly access, landscaping and periodic maintenance. The nearby Keta Market presents an even stronger opportunity. It is a major commercial centre and a natural source of customers for savings, credit, payments, insurance and digital banking. GCB could establish a Keta Market Enterprise and Financial Inclusion Programme focused on financial literacy, affordable working capital for credible traders, digital-payment adoption, microinsurance, pension education, fire and flood preparedness, sanitation and targeted support for women- and youth-owned enterprises. This would not be charity alone; it would be responsible banking and sound commercial strategy. Responsibility Must Extend Beyond One Institution The call for responsible corporate citizenship should not be directed at GCB Bank alone. All major hotels, financial institutions, telecommunications companies, fuel stations, supermarkets, construction companies and other large businesses operating in Keta and Anlo must become intentional about their corporate social responsibility investments. Businesses cannot continue to derive revenue, labour, land, patronage and goodwill from a community while treating local development as somebody else’s responsibility. Every major enterprise operating in the area should maintain a clear annual CSR plan, developed in consultation with local stakeholders and linked to measurable priorities such as market infrastructure, sanitation, education, health, youth employment, coastal resilience, disaster relief and support for small businesses. CSR must not be reduced to occasional donations, ceremonial appearances or publicity-driven gestures. It must be planned, budgeted, sustained and publicly accountable. A Disaster-Prone Community Requires Responsive Institutions Keta and neighbouring coastal communities have repeatedly experienced tidal waves, coastal erosion, flooding, displacement and damage to livelihoods and public infrastructure. These shocks directly affect customers, workers, market activity and local businesses. During such periods, communities are entitled to expect solidarity and practical support from the major institutions that operate among them. GCB Bank and other large businesses could jointly support a Keta-Anlo Disaster Resilience and Recovery Facility to provide emergency relief, concessionary recovery finance for viable businesses, assistance to affected schools and health facilities, and support for properly governed community resilience initiatives. A responsible business should not become visible only when it is mobilising deposits, selling rooms, marketing services or collecting revenue; it should also be visible when the community faces distress. Leadership, Accountability and a New Standard Institutional neglect is not always caused by a lack of resources. Sometimes it reflects weak local leadership, excessive centralisation, poor stakeholder engagement or a narrow focus on commercial targets. Branch managers, hotel managers and corporate executives should be assessed not only on revenue and profitability, but also on customer satisfaction, environmental responsibility, local enterprise support and measurable community impact. This is not a call for hostility towards GCB Bank or any investor. It is a call for a higher standard of corporate conduct. Keta and Anlo welcome legitimate investment, but investment must be responsible. Commercial presence without meaningful local partnership is no longer enough. GCB Bank should convene a stakeholder forum involving the Keta Municipal Assembly, traditional authorities, market associations, educational and health institutions, youth and women’s groups, businesses and customers. From that engagement, the Bank could adopt a five-year Keta and Anlo Community Impact Programme built around market modernisation, financial inclusion, disaster resilience, education, youth enterprise and environmental improvement. Other major businesses should be encouraged to adopt similar programmes. Conclusion Keta and Anlo have supported major institutions and businesses for decades. It is now appropriate for those institutions to demonstrate, consistently and visibly, that they also believe in the resilience, enterprise and future of the area. Responsible investment must mean more than operating in a community; it must mean contributing deliberately to the dignity, prosperity and sustainability of that community. The time has come to move from commercial presence to meaningful partnership—and communities must firmly demand nothing less.

items.title: Kindia : des familles sinistrées après les fortes pluies, les riverains interpellent les autorités
items.description: Les fortes pluies qui se sont abattues sur la ville de Kindia dans la journée du dimanche 19 juillet 2026 ont causé d'importants dégâts matériels dans plusieurs quartiers de la commune urbaine. Dans la matinée de ce lundi 20 juillet 2026, notre correspondant basé à Kindia s'est rendu sur le terrain pour constater l'ampleur des dommages dans plusieurs concessions envahies par les eaux de ruissellement. Les quartiers Yéolé, Caravanserail, Gadha Wawa, Abattoir, Cacia, entre autres, ont été durement touchés. Au centre-ville, la montée rapide des eaux a paralysé plusieurs axes de circulation. À Caravanserail, une famille a perdu une grande partie de ses biens. Les habitants pointent notamment du doigt les travaux de construction inachevés. « Ce qui nous est arrivé hier était vraiment pitoyable. Nous étions en train de préparer du riz lorsqu'il s'est mis à pleuvoir avec une rare intensité. Avant même de terminer la cuisson, notre concession était déjà inondée. Nos bols ont été emportés, les sacs de riz mouillés, les lits et plusieurs autres biens ont été endommagés. Tout cela est dû aux travaux de construction du pont, laissés inachevés. Du sable a été déposé derrière notre cour, empêchant l'eau de s'écouler normalement. Les autorités doivent achever ce pont, sinon nous ne savons plus quoi faire », témoigne Aminata Camara, habitante du quartier Caravanserail. Si certains riverains accusent le retard dans l'achèvement des ponts situés sur les cours d'eau de Takhou et de Wawa, d'autres dénoncent également l'occupation anarchique des zones inondables. « Cette inondation nous a énormément affectés. Nous avons passé une nuit très difficile, notamment à cause de la prolifération des moustiques. Nous sommes installés ici depuis 1980 et c'est la première fois que l'eau atteint notre concession. Aujourd'hui, les citoyens ne respectent plus les lits des marigots. Les constructions anarchiques se multiplient et les 40 mètres réglementaires ne sont plus respectés. Tout cela se passe sous les yeux des autorités. Si rien n'est fait, Kindia risque de faire face à une situation encore plus grave », alerte Lansana Boukhari Camara. Dans plusieurs concessions, les habitants ont passé toute la nuit à évacuer l'eau. Les dégâts sont considérables et touchent également les activités agricoles. Sur son champ, en train de nettoyer les débris laissés par les eaux, Meli Sylla, maraîchère, exprime son désarroi. « Nous avons subi d'importantes pertes. Tout le champ a été envahi par les eaux et des déchets ont recouvert nos cultures. C'est de ce champ que nous vivons. Nous investissons dans les semences, les engrais et la main-d'œuvre. Si les eaux emportent tout, nous ne savons plus comment nous en sortir. Nous lançons un appel aux autorités et aux personnes de bonne volonté afin qu'elles nous viennent en aide », a-t-elle déclaré. Contrairement aux années précédentes, la montée des eaux suscite une vive inquiétude chez les habitants des quartiers sinistrés, qui appellent les autorités à prendre des mesures urgentes pour prévenir de nouvelles catastrophes. Aboubacar Dramé, correspondant régional à Kindia +224 623 08 09 10 L’article Kindia : des familles sinistrées après les fortes pluies, les riverains interpellent les autorités est apparu en premier sur Mediaguinee.com .

items.title: PML-N MPA seeks ban on appointment of dual nationals to government service
items.description: LAHORE: A resolution has been submitted to the Punjab Assembly seeking a ban on the appointment of dual nationals to government service, arguing that holding citizenship of another country raises concerns over allegiance and access to sensitive state information. The resolution, moved by ruling PML-N MPA Amjad Ali Javed, calls on the federal and Punjab governments to enact legislation barring dual nationals from obtaining government jobs. According to the text of the resolution, an investigative report claimed that around 24,000 serving employees in various government departments held dual nationality. It stated that many of their family members also possessed foreign citizenship, while others were in the process of acquiring it. The resolution argued that acquiring citizenship of another country requires an oath of allegiance to that state, which may raise questions about an individual’s loyalty to Pakistan. It noted that, on similar grounds, dual nationals are already barred from contesting elections. It further stated that government officials are entrusted with sensitive information relating to national security, have access to important official records and play a key role in administrative and policy decisions. However, existing laws do not prevent dual nationals from serving in government positions. The resolution urged both governments to introduce appropriate legislation and take necessary administrative measures to prohibit dual nationals from securing public sector employment, saying such steps were essential to safeguard national interests and protect sensitive state institutions. In January, JUI-F MNA Noor Alam Khan also called for barring bureaucrats from holding dual nationality. “And if this does not happen, parliamentarians should also be allowed to have dual citizenship,” he said, in a meeting of the National Assembly Standing Committee on Cabinet Secretariat, where the Civil Servants (Amendment) Bill, 2024 — which was moved by Khan — was taken up.

items.title: Élections en Colombie et au Pérou : quelles leçons politiques pour l’Amérique latine ?
items.description: L’Amérique latine connaît une recomposition politique marquée par le recul des gouvernements progressistes au profit de droites conservatrices et radicales. Les élections présidentielles organisées en juin 2026 en Colombie et au Pérou ne font pas exception et s’inscrivent pleinement dans cette dynamique. En Colombie, l’outsider d’extrême droite Bernardo Della Espriella s’est imposé face au candidat de gauche Iván Cepeda. Au Pérou, Keiko Fujimori, figure de la droite conservatrice péruvienne, a remporté l’élection face à Roberto Sánchez. Dans les deux pays, les nouveaux dirigeants ont été élus avec une très faible avance sur leur adversaire, donnant lieu à des contestations des résultats de la part des candidats battus. Ces deux nouveaux gouvernements de droite radicale ont des orientations politiques proches, notamment sur les plans économique, sécuritaire, sociétal et diplomatique. Comment expliquer l’ascension des droites radicales en Amérique latine ? Dans quelle mesure l’absence de majorité parlementaire pourrait-elle limiter leur capacité à gouverner ? Comment ces nouvelles présidences pourraient-elles redéfinir les orientations économiques, sécuritaires, diplomatiques et sociétales de la Colombie et du Pérou ? Nouvelle chronique de l'Amérique latine avec Christophe Ventura, directeur de recherche à l'IRIS et responsable du Programme Amérique latine/Caraïbe. L’article Élections en Colombie et au Pérou : quelles leçons politiques pour l’Amérique latine ? est apparu en premier sur IRIS .

items.title: La Bourse de Casablanca finit sur une note négative
items.description: La Bourse de Casablanca a fini sur une note négative lundi, son indice principal, le MASI, cédant 0,22% à 17.539,62 points (pts). Le MASI 20, qui regroupe les 20 valeurs les plus liquides, a diminué de 0,11% à 1.301,16 pts et le MASI ESG, indice des entreprises ayant la meilleure notation ESG selon Moody's ESG Solutions, a reculé de 0,06% à 1.243,72 pts. Le MASI Mid and Small Cap, reflétant la performance des petites et moyennes entreprises cotées, a abandonné 0,22% à 1.748,02 pts. Au volet international, le FTSE CSE Morocco 15 Index et le FTSE CSE Morocco All-Liquid ont clôturé sur des gains respectifs de 0,41% à 17.051,3 pts et de 0,29% à 15.161,59 pts. Sur le plan sectoriel, les indices « Sociétés de portefeuilles – Holdings » (-3,61%), « Transport » (-2,48%) et « Pétrole et gaz » (-2,12%) ont accusé les plus fortes baisses. À l'inverse, les secteurs « Électricité » (+0,88%), « Télécommunications » (+0,67%) et « Sylviculture et papier » (+0,56%) ont signé les meilleures performances. Les échanges ont dépassé 87,9 millions de dirhams (MDH), réalisés entièrement sur le marché central « Actions » et dominés par les transactions sur CIH (16,1 MDH), Attijariwafa Bank (11,05 MDH) et TGCC (9,11 MDH). La capitalisation boursière s'est établie à plus de 1.002,84 milliards de dirhams. Aux valeurs individuelles, les plus forts replis ont été enregistrés par Sanlam Maroc (-5,51% à 2.881 DH), Microdata (-4,94% à 712 DH), Réalisations Mécaniques (-4,01% à 450 DH), TotalEnergies Marketing Maroc (-3,69% à 1.435 DH) et Delta Holding (-3,64% à 53 DH). Contre-tendance, Rebab Company (+6% à 93,29 DH), Balima (+5,99% à 199,9 DH), Involys (+5,79% à 128 DH), Sonasid (+2,94% à 1.999 DH) et Jet Contractors (+2,9% à 2.058 DH) ont affiché les plus fortes hausses. The post La Bourse de Casablanca finit sur une note négative appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .
