items.title: ’Turning Up the Heat on the Streets’
items.description: Subscribe here: Apple Podcasts | Spotify | YouTube | Overcast | Pocket Casts Friday, Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin was asked at a press conference about his previous comments of “turning down the temperature.” This was a reasonable question given that two men, one in Texas and one in Maine, were recently shot and killed by immigration agents who approached them in their cars. A few days earlier, following conversations with Republican Senator Susan Collins of Maine, Mullin had said he was temporarily suspending traffic stops, which certainly sounded like a temperature turndown. But then Donald Trump promptly stomped on that idea: “We CANNOT give up on I.C.E.’s most important and effective Crime Fighting tools, THE TRAFFIC STOP!” the president wrote on Truth Social. So in response to the question about temperature Friday, Mullin gave the answer his boss required: “I turn down the temperature with you guys,” he said, pointing to the reporters, and continued, “we’re turning up the heat on the streets.” Right now immigration enforcement is in a combustible place. Both of the recent killings involved traffic stops, which immigration officers have less experience and training in than other law enforcement. The Maine killing involved a new recruit who, according to emerging reports, had a history of violent behavior and serious mental-health issues. Since the killing of two American citizens in Minnesota in January, approval for Trump’s immigration-enforcement tactics has dipped. And yet he is getting pressure from his supporters to stay firm. Despite the recent killings, the administration says it is still trying for 2,000 immigration arrests a day. Our guest today is the staff writer Nick Miroff, who covers immigration. We talk about the details of the shootings, the problem with the traffic stop, and the direction Mullin seems to be taking the agency. The following is a transcript of the episode: Nick Miroff: These ICE officers are swooping in out of nowhere, in some cases trying to pin the vehicle of the person that they want to arrest. And, you know, if they do this hundreds of times a day across the country in multiple cities, it stands to reason that in some cases the people they’re after are going to panic. And if they panic and try to drive away, does that, you know, justify killing them, you know? And the answer is no, of course, but it points to the inherent risks of these kinds of tactics by ICE officers. Hanna Rosin: The recent killings of two men—Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Texas and Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero in Maine—by immigration officers have put the Department of Homeland Security back in the place that Secretary Markwayne Mullin was trying to avoid. Markwayne Mullin (from his Senate confirmation hearing on March 18): My goal, in six months, is that we’re not in the lead story every single day. So, the administration didn’t want to be on the front page, but it didn’t want to give up on its aggressive immigration agenda either. Recently it’s ramped up immigration arrests to 2,000 a day. And Friday Mullin said they are staying the course. Mullin (from his press conference on July 17): We’re turning up the heat on the streets. We’re out there working harder than we ever have because we’ve empowered our law enforcement to do their job. Many of the recent arrests were traffic stops—that’s how the men in Texas and Maine were killed. And in Maine, the officer involved was a new recruit with, reports say, a history of violent behavior and serious mental-health issues. So they are definitely back in the headlines. I’m Hanna Rosin. This is Radio Atlantic . Joining me today to talk about the two recent killings and about DHS Secretary Mullin, is Nick Miroff, who covers immigration for The Atlantic . Rosin: Hey Nick. Welcome to the show. Miroff: Thanks. Good to be with you. Rosin: So after Renee Good and Alex Pretti were shot and killed in Minnesota, there was a lot of pushback against the way immigration enforcement was being done, including by Republicans, including by some MAGA people. So how did we get back here again with two killings by immigration officers in such a short period? Miroff: Yeah, well, there was a kind of a broader pullback, right? I mean, the administration sent Tom Homan into Minneapolis to clean things up. The president ousted Kristi Noem. Markwayne Mullin came in as the new DHS secretary, signaling that he wanted things to be quieter, to keep DHS out of the headlines. And yet the calls on the right started, you know, throughout the spring were growing for them to intensify their ICE operations. People were seeing the ICE arrests and deportation numbers lower than they had been prior to Minneapolis. And Markwayne Mullin as well as “border czar” Tom Homan were getting accused on the right of backing off of Trump’s promises to carry out the largest, you know, deportation campaign in U.S. history. And so what we’ve seen in the last few weeks was a rapid intensification of ICE activity. They have been arresting about twice as many people as they were doing a few months ago. And they were trying to do it more quietly. It didn’t look like Minneapolis, because it wasn’t all concentrated in one city. It wasn’t Border Patrol agents, you know, sweeping through neighborhoods. It was the kind of targeted enforcement that Tom Homan signaled that he wanted, to double down on the kind of tactics that ICE has always done. But this time it’s been more focused on vehicle stops, not on homes. It’s on vehicle stops. And these vehicle stops are inherently dangerous for many of the reasons that we have just seen. Rosin: What do we know about the details of these shootings so far? Because we haven’t had the kind of videos emerge like we did in Minnesota, where we could see clearly what happened at the moment of shooting. Miroff: You know, I’d say one thing else about that, which is, you know, if you think about Minneapolis, because every everyone was so stirred up and the protesters were out in the streets, it was almost impossible for ICE to carry out some kind of arrest like this without somebody filming it on a cellphone. Says a lot about how much more quietly they’ve been able to operate since Minneapolis. And the focus on the body cams, you know, is once again become a contentious matter. ICE got, you know, tens of billions of dollars obviously last year through the One Big Beautiful Bill [Act] and got another several tens of billions through the reconciliation bill that passed prior to the shutdown. So the White House is saying it’s the Democrats’ fault that the body cameras haven’t been distributed across the country, but Democrats are pointing out that they had tens of billions of dollars at their disposal, you know, last summer that they could have used on body cameras. And Kristi Noem after Minneapolis said that they were gonna get body cameras out to ICE officers around the country. And here we are six months later with no body-camera footage of either fatal shooting. Rosin: What do we know about each of these incidents? Because it sounds like the traffic-stop element is important. So, so what do we know? Miroff: Well, I mean what’s, I think, you know, one of the first things that’s significant about both incidents is that the officers killed someone who wasn’t the target of their arrest operation that day. So in Houston, the team that went out and decided to pull over the work van driven by Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, the 52-year-old construction contractor and father of three U.S. citizens. In that case, they were looking for somebody who they have not named, and they were doing the kind of targeted enforcement that they’ve been doing across the country. Typically they go early in the morning to try to catch people as they’re coming out of their homes and getting into their cars or heading off to work. You know, the tactics that ICE has relied on historically to get people at their homes—that doesn’t work anymore. People no longer open their doors for ICE. They know that officers need a judicial warrant in order to force their way into a home. And so ICE has kind of given up on that tactic and they’re trying to catch people as they’re leaving for work, and that’s what they were doing with this work van, even though Mr. Salgado was not the target of their operation. And so what we know is that they tried to pull him over. There’s surveillance-camera footage showing that he didn’t obey their commands, that he continued to drive. His sons have said that they thought he probably panicked. He was worried that he was getting robbed for his work tools that were his livelihood. So he didn’t stop, and they tried to box him in. The Department of Homeland Security said that he tried to weaponize his vehicle and drive at them. Reporters who went to the scene did not see any major impacts on any of the vehicles suggesting that there was a big crash. And there are eyewitness accounts claiming that the ICE officer who shot Salgado was reaching through the open passenger window, shot him in the stomach, and then the officers pulled him out of the vehicle and tried to handcuff him. There’s a bystander video recorded that shows two officers over him, and he’s moaning in pain. We know he was taken to the hospital after that, where he died. Rosin: You mentioned that Salgado’s sons said he might have panicked when the police tried to stop him because he thought they were trying to rob him. That’s not what a lot of people would picture as a traffic stop. Like, you would see the police lights flashing; you would know that it was a police car. So, what is a traffic stop like by an immigration-enforcement agency? Miroff: Yeah, that’s a great question. These ICE vehicle stops are not like traffic stops conducted by normal police officers with marked cars and flashing lights. These are ICE officers, typically in plain clothes driving in unmarked cars. And they’re really relying on the element of surprise. It isn’t like driving past a police car on the highway and then it turns on its lights and you don’t understand why you’re being pulled over. These ICE officers are swooping in out of nowhere, in some cases trying to pin the vehicle of the person that they want to arrest. And, you know, if they do this hundreds of times a day across the country in multiple cities, it stands to reason that in some cases, the people they’re after are going to panic. And if they panic and try to drive away, does that, you know, justify killing them, you know? And the answer is no, of course, but it points to the inherent risks of these kinds of tactics by ICE officers. Rosin: So that’s Texas. What about the incident in Maine? Miroff: We saw another targeted enforcement operation gone wrong in the town of Bitterford, Maine. And in that incident, what we know is that a team of officers went to a residence looking for someone who had what’s called a final order of removal; an immigration judge had ordered them to leave the country. But it was not the person that ICE ended up shooting. And so, a Colombian father named Joan Sebastián Guerrero was on his way to work coming out of his home. Again, the officers claim that he used his vehicle in a way that they described as a threat to public safety. We’ve seen security footage showing the aftermath of the shooting, in which the vehicle is kind of doing loops in an intersection after they have shot him. The officer involved fired at least four shots through the windshield. He was struck in the head. There’s also footage showing them taking him out of the vehicle and attempting to handcuff him as he’s dying. And we know that the officer who fired was a new hire—in this case, an officer who had federal law-enforcement experience working for the Veterans Affairs Department Police Force, but had been hired by ICE just this year. And I think, you know, more details are coming out about this officer’s training and his background. Rosin: Yeah, and those details are pretty alarming. Members of his family told the media that he had a history of mental-health issues dating back to when he was a preteen and also a history of violent behaviors. So that’s that one officer in Maine. Now more broadly, another thing that’s come up a lot is traffic stops. Both of these killings involved traffic stops. So what’s the significance of that? Is that something that these immigration officers are not particularly trained to handle? Miroff: Yeah. What I’ve been told is that, you know, ICE officers not only went through this kind of fast-track training program last fall and into this winter, because of the White House’s urgency in getting, you know, thousands of more trainees out onto the streets; that generally ICE officers don’t get as much training in safe vehicle stops as, say, large police departments and that at the same time they are increasingly relying on this tactic in order to make their arrest numbers and to meet the pressure from the White House to have more and more arrests and deportations. And so they’re relying on this tactic. They don’t have what police departments call a vehicle pursuit policy, that really establishes, like, clear guidelines as to when you can use force during a vehicle stop. Because what the Supreme Court has established is that you cannot shoot somebody just because they’re disobeying your commands or they’re fleeing police officers. They have to—the officer has to be able to articulate, you know, a reasonable fear of imminent harm to themself, to the other officers, or to the public. Rosin: When we come back, more about what we can learn about how DHS is operating and the agency’s new secretary, Markwayne Mullin. [ Break ] Rosin: Now, what happened after these killings is confusing, and I think clearing it up will help us understand where immigration policy is headed. The way it was first reported is that Markwayne Mullin talked to Susan Collins, the senator of Maine, issued a policy to at least temporarily halt these traffic stops that you’re talking about. And then Trump comes out on Truth Social and seems sort of incensed by this. What did Trump write, and how do you explain what happened? Miroff: Yeah. Well, the order of events is that Secretary Mullin talked to Susan Collins three times on Monday after the fatal shooting in Maine, and he also talked to Senator Angus King, the other senator from Maine. He had been hearing from senior ICE officials that the vehicle stops were becoming a problem and are exposing the insufficient training of these officers. So he was already hearing about these, about these worries. And so late Monday night, an order went out from an ICE official to all the ICE supervisors around the country to halt the vehicle stops. And so the president—you know, people woke up to this Tuesday and by, you know, Wednesday morning the president sent out his his statement on Truth Social, effectively saying that they needed to continue with the traffic stops, that—kind of countermanding the order from the ICE officials and and saying that the traffic stops are a much-needed tool and that they can’t back down. And we know that he did that because throughout the day on Tuesday as, you know, hard-line—immigration hard-liners and members of the, you know, more extreme members of the MAGA coalition were telling him that it would make him look weak. And then kind of saying similar things to what they said after Minneapolis was that, you know, They’re giving in to protesters to the radical left, they can’t back down from the mass deportation campaign that Trump has promised . And, you know, clearly they broke through to him and he has reversed course. Now, you know, what we’re still seeing is that some kind of new guidance is coming to ICE officers that is going to try to introduce some additional safeguards into these vehicle stops. But it also shows that the president is very sensitive to criticism from his base about any perceived backtracking on this deportation campaign. Rosin: Yeah, ’cause you said it calmly, but it was a lot of capitals and exclamation points. I mean, it seemed like a very aggressive statement the president was making, which doesn’t necessarily clarify the confusion, because I’m assuming that Mullin wouldn’t have issued that statement without checking with someone at the White House. So you think just afterwards there was a lot of MAGA pressure to reverse course, and it’s as simple as that? Miroff: It was telling that on Wednesday, senior administration officials were really throwing Mullin under the bus on this one and saying that he hadn’t cleared this, you know, vehicle-stop order with the White House. But I also spoke to people in the department who said it was—you know, that wasn’t possible, that there’s no way he would have cleared this kind of thing. He would have—in this kind of order would have gone forward without people, including, you know, Tom Homan and Stephen Miller from being aware that they were, that they were—that it was coming. Rosin: Mullin is still in charge for the moment. Has he made any significant changes? How is he different than the previous leadership? Miroff : Well, Mullin came in to replace Kristi Noem, who, along with her top adviser, Corey Lewandowski, ran the department in a very different way and made a lot of enemies there, generated a lot of controversy, and ultimately were removed because of the perception that she was trying to promote her own career, her own interests, and and had just become too much of a liability for the White House in a way that was undermining the president’s broader immigration-enforcement goals. You know, Mullin was brought in, I think, to stabilize the department, to bring a kind of a sunnier presence while still being somebody who was close to the president and intensely loyal to Trump and who would carry out, you know, the the campaign with a better face, so to speak. But, you know, their goal has been to carry out the president’s mass-deportation campaign but more quietly and with less controversy. And I think that they had been relatively effective in doing that up until these shootings. But it really shows the inherent, you know, tension in what they’re trying to do. They’re trying to carry out this incredibly disruptive, intrusive, controversial, you know, deportation campaign in American cities and communities and there’s just no way it’s gonna be quiet and that people aren’t gonna push back. Rosin: So do you have a sense that the two latest shootings could change policy at all—could shift the course of how they’re doing immigration enforcement? Miroff: I think that after the president’s response to that order and his fear of looking weak, I think no. They’re gonna continue pressing forward. They have the money; they have the personnel. Trump has made this commitment. This is like the signature domestic-policy initiative of his second term. So they’re going to go forward and they’re gonna try to, you know, keep a lid on this stuff politically as best they can and the question is gonna be, you know, can they pull it off? How many more of these shootings can Americans stomach? Rosin: Well, Nick, thank you for joining us today. Miroff: Thank you. Anytime, Hanna. Rosin: That’s all for today’s show. This episode of Radio Atlantic was produced by Mike Pesoli. It was engineered and has original music by Rob Smierciak. Claudine Ebeid is the executive producer of Atlantic audio, and Andrea Valdez is our managing editor. If you like what you saw here, new episodes of Radio Atlantic drop every Monday and Thursday. You can subscribe on The Atlantic ’s YouTube page, on Apple or Spotify, or wherever you get your podcasts. And if you want to support this work and the work of my colleagues, you can subscribe to this publication at TheAtlantic.com/Listener . Until next time, I’m Hanna Rosin.

items.title: KP approaches FCC, challenges years-long extension of 7th NFC Award after Fata merger
items.description: ISLAMABAD: With a staggering Rs964 billion claim on the Federal Consolidated Fund (FCF), the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa government has filed a constitutional petition in the Federal Constitutional Court (FCC), challenging the continued extension of the 7th National Finance Commission (NFC) Award despite the merger of the erstwhile Federally Administered Tribal Areas into the province in 2018, it emerged on Monday. In its petition, the KP government asked the FCC to determine whether its share under the NFC should be increased to reflect its expanded size and responsibilities following the 25th Constitutional Amendment , which merged the Federally Administered Tribal Areas (Fata) into the province. The petition argues that the merger significantly increased the province’s population, territorial expanse and development needs, warranting a corresponding increase in its share under the NFC Award. It argued that despite this major constitutional change, its share under the NFC formula had not been updated to account for the merged districts. “This violates the global principle of ‘finance follows functions’, resulting in an under-allocation of resources for the additional population of the merged districts in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa,” the petition said. If the court decides in the province’s favour, it may, to a large extent, nullify criticism of the 18th Amendment. This petition goes beyond a provincial fiscal claim and asks the FCC to clarify whether, in light of Article 160, the federal government’s claim of a constitutional logjam is justified or amounts to a refusal to fulfil its constitutional role. In its petition, KP maintained that the existing NFC formula agreed under the 7th NFC Award did not need to be reopened or altered for the purpose of its present claim. Rather, it argued that the formula should be applied to the province as it constitutionally existed after the 25th Amendment. “The formula continues to be used, but the values inserted into that formula still reflect the pre-merger Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. This means that the population, geography and development indicators of the merged districts have not been included in the share of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, despite the fact that they became part of the Province by constitutional amendment on May 31, 2018,” the petition said. Through its petition, the KP government also sought a revision of its share in the horizontal distribution of the Provincial Divisible Pool with effect from the date of the merger. “The province’s position is that this was not a discretionary grant, nor a political concession, but the automatic constitutional consequence of the 25th Amendment when read with the existing NFC formula.” The petition also sought a declaration that KP’s entitlement was payable from the date the 25th Amendment came into force in 2018 . The province argued that this did not involve retrospectivity. Its case is that the relevant constitutional facts changed on May 31, 2018, and that the law already in force on that date — namely, the formula under the 2010 Order — automatically applied to those changed facts. The province has therefore sought recognition of an accrued constitutional entitlement, not the retrospective application of a later legal instrument. Based on the calculation placed before the court, KP’s claim arising from the failure to update its horizontal share stands at Rs964 billion. The province maintains that this amount has instead flowed to other provinces because the post-merger constitutional reality was not reflected in the NFC distribution framework. The petition argued that article 160 envisaged a “periodic, deliberative and evidence-based framework” for fiscal federalism through the NFC to be constituted at intervals not exceeding five years so that the distribution of national resources remains responsive to changing constitutional, demographic, fiscal and developmental realities. “The continued reliance on arrangements rooted in the 7th NFC Award, without adequately reflecting the merger of FATA with Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, has created an unresolved constitutional and fiscal anomaly,” it said, adding that the case did not seek to undermine the federation or reduce the NFC to an adversarial contest among provinces. On the contrary, the province approached the court precisely because the constitutional mechanism requires clarity, discipline and implementation in good faith. Fiscal federalism can function only if the agreed constitutional formula is applied to the federating units as they exist under the Constitution. The province asked the court to determine important questions concerning the scope and spirit of Article 160, including: Whether the Constitution requires equitable distribution of financial resources among the federation and provinces. Whether such equitable distribution is a fundamental feature of Pakistan’s federal scheme. Whether each NFC is constitutionally required to independently review and recommend the distribution of revenues, and Whether an award founded on the recommendations of an earlier Commission can continue indefinitely without fresh recommendations by a subsequently constituted Commission. The petition also sought clarity on the relationship between Article 160(4) and Article 160(6) of the Constitution, including whether the president’s power to amend an NFC Order is a power coupled with a duty when constitutional, demographic or fiscal circumstances materially change. KP also asked the court to declare whether provincial shares are automatically adjusted by operation of law when the fundamental basis on which they were calculated changes, and whether the federal government and the president are constitutionally bound to give effect to such a change through an Amendment and Modification Order under Article 160(6). It argued that, in numerous instances since 1973, Article 160(6) had been invoked to give effect to changing demographic and other realities by updating the existing formula and revising provincial shares. It also asked the FCC to declare that the president is constitutionally bound to promulgate, and that the federal government is constitutionally bound to advise the president to promulgate, an Amendment and Modification Order under Article 160(6) of the Constitution to give effect to the province’s revised share. The province’s separate and independent position regarding the national commitment to the development and integration of the merged districts — including the commitment of Rs100 billion per annum from 2018, or an allocation equivalent to 3pc of the Divisible Pool — remains distinct. “By seeking clarity on the duties of the federation, the president and the NFC framework, the petition aimed to restore constitutional discipline, fairness and good-faith implementation to one of the central mechanisms of Pakistan’s federal compact,” said Muzammil Aslam, advisor to the chief minister on finance, during a press conference. This was in addition to the Rs100 billion-per-annum national commitment made in 2018 for the development and merger of the merged districts, which has not been fully implemented, he added. “As such, KP’s total claims go beyond Rs1.6 trillion.” “At stake is not only Pakhtunkhwa’s share, but also the integrity of Article 160 itself: whether the NFC remains a periodic, evidence-based and responsive constitutional mechanism, or whether national resource distribution can continue on outdated assumptions after a province’s population, territory and responsibilities have been transformed,” Aslam said.

items.title: Plateau Killings: BECO demands justice for two women, calls for probe into security operation
items.description: By Golok Nanmwa JOS — The Berom Educational and Cultural Organisation (BECO) Worldwide has called for a thorough investigation into the death of two women allegedly killed during a security operation in Gero community, Jos South Local Government Area of Plateau State. The organisation, while condemning the incident, declared that “enough is enough” over what it described as recurring violence and insecurity affecting communities in the state. The incident, which occurred on Monday, triggered tension in the area as residents protested, with reports of vandalism at parts of the Jos South Local Government Secretariat. The two women were reportedly shot dead behind the council secretariat in Gero, although the circumstances surrounding their deaths remained unclear as of the time of filing this report. Some community members have alleged that security operatives were responsible for the killings, but the claim has not been independently verified. Speaking with journalists in Jos, the President of BECO Worldwide, Da Gyang Dudu Dalyop, called for an impartial investigation to determine what transpired, alleging that the incident may have resulted from poor communication, inadequate community engagement and excessive use of force. Dalyop said residents of the affected communities have endured years of attacks and insecurity, making them particularly sensitive to unfamiliar movements, especially those involving armed personnel. He explained that the security personnel were reportedly in the area for an operation but that residents became suspicious because they were allegedly not informed about the purpose of the mission. According to him, proper engagement with community leaders before the operation could have prevented the misunderstanding. “The people have been living with constant attacks. They have gone through deep crisis and are very sensitive to any movement they do not understand. There should have been proper sensitisation and explanation to the community,” he said. The BECO president alleged that the security operatives fired warning shots before the women were killed, questioning the use of force against civilians. “I do not see any reason why maximum force should be used on women who were not carrying weapons or posing any threat. We believe there was an overuse of power and this must be investigated,” he added. Dalyop urged the Commissioner of Police, the Department of State Services (DSS), military authorities and other relevant security agencies to investigate the incident and establish the facts surrounding the deaths. He also raised concerns over coordination among security agencies, stressing the need for better communication with community leaders and local stakeholders before security operations are carried out. The BECO leader appealed to the government to intensify efforts to address insecurity in Plateau State, warning that continued violence was creating frustration among residents. “We have had more than enough. We are losing people and the youths are becoming restless. We plead with government to tackle insecurity,” he said. Dalyop disclosed that two women died in the incident, while another person was receiving treatment in a hospital in critical condition. He urged authorities to ensure that future security operations involve traditional rulers, community leaders and other stakeholders to prevent misunderstandings and promote cooperation between security agencies and communities. “We are all partners in security. Nobody should treat security in isolation. Community leaders must be carried along whenever there is any form of deployment,” he said. The post Plateau Killings: BECO demands justice for two women, calls for probe into security operation appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Iran says fighting 'full-scale war' with United States
items.description: Iran is fighting a “full-scale war” with the United States, its president said on Monday, as Washington expanded its strikes across the Islamic republic including a city hosting a nuclear power plant. Iran kept up its own attacks across the region in response, with its Revolutionary Guards announcing they had hit targets in Bahrain, Jordan and Syria, while Bahrain said Tehran had targeted its air navigation systems. “The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” said President Masoud Pezeshkian, according to a presidency statement. He added that “we must be realistic and accept the natural consequences of this resistance”. His comments came after state media and local authorities reported US strikes on Monday in the north and centre of the country, further than areas near the blockaded Strait of Hormuz waterway through which a fifth of world oil supplies transited prior to the war. The Middle East war resumed after a weeks-long lull prompted by an agreement signed in June, and oil prices surged to a one-month high as the foes battled to control the vital Strait of Hormuz. As its truce with the US collapsed, Tehran reimposed its blockade of the waterway, while Washington hit back by again blockading Iran’s ports. All of the Iranian provinces hit on Monday host military bases and air defence systems. In Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power plant, governor Mohammad Mozaffari told state news agency IRNA : “A few minutes ago, several locations in Bushehr city were hit by American enemy’s projectiles.” In East Azerbaijan province in the north, the head of the local crisis management unit Majid Farshi told IRNA that “one person was martyred and several others injured” in an attack there. Despite the latest attacks, Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said diplomatic exchanges with the United States via mediators were ongoing. “Ideas have been conveyed to us by certain mediators,” he told a press conference in Tehran attended by AFP . Iran’s Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni was set to travel to Islamabad on Monday, a Pakistani interior ministry source told AFP , without confirming the reason. Ship on fire In Hormuz, meanwhile, sailors abandoned a vessel set on fire in an attack, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency. The Revolutionary Guards had earlier said that two oil tankers trying to transit the strait “exploded and were brought to a halt”, according to a statement reported by Tasnim news agency. On Sunday, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio called on countries around the world to share the burden of protecting global shipping. But Baqaei said on Monday that Tehran would not allow the strait to be used by its foes “to threaten Iran’s security and national interests”. US Central Command (Centcom) said on Sunday that it successfully completed the ninth consecutive night of strikes against Iran, while Rubio said he would “continue to try” to reach a diplomatic solution. The fresh fighting came after a weekend in which the US announced three new US military fatalities and one service member missing in action. Two were killed during Iranian missile and drone attacks on Jordan on Friday, while another service member died in northern Iraq during the controlled detonation of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian drone, Centcom said. President Donald Trump said US strikes on Iran “hit them very hard” in honour of the American fatalities. The deaths brought to 17 the confirmed number of American military fatalities since the conflict began with a massive US-Israeli strike on the Islamic republic. On Friday, Iran’s health ministry reported that 50 people had been killed in recent fighting and more than 500 wounded. Iran strikes neighbours Meanwhile, Iran’s Revolutionary Guards announced on Monday that they had hit targets in Syria, Jordan and Kuwait overnight. In Syria, Iranian forces launched “a surprise attack on the enemy special operations command centre in the Al-Tanf region”, according to a Guards statement carried by state media. The US withdrew its troops from Al-Tanf in February. In Jordan, the Guards said it targeted US military aircraft stationed at Aqaba Airport “with ballistic missiles, causing severe damage to a number of them”. The Guards also said they hit US military targets based in Kuwait, including an early-warning radar system that was “completely destroyed”. Kuwaiti air defences were “engaging hostile drone attacks following Iranian aggression”, its army said on Monday. Bahrain’s interior ministry also said that its air raid sirens had sounded and urged citizens and residents to head to “the nearest safe place”.

items.title: 10 countries in Europe that give visas easily to Africans
items.description: In recent years, an increasing number of Africans have sought opportunities in Europe through study, work, business and long-term residence programmes. While many are pursuing higher education, skilled employment or entrepreneurship, others are looking to build new lives through legal immigration pathways offered by several European countries. Despite these opportunities, the visa application process remains one of the biggest hurdles for many prospective migrants. Strict documentation requirements, financial proof, language requirements and varying immigration policies often make the process challenging. However, some European countries have developed relatively transparent and well-defined immigration systems for qualified international applicants. Below are 10 European countries that offer established pathways for Africans to study, work or settle, provided they meet the relevant eligibility requirements. 1. Germany Germany is one of Europe’s leading destinations for skilled workers and international students, offering the EU Blue Card, Opportunity Card, work visas and residence permits for qualified professionals. 2. Portugal Portugal provides multiple legal migration routes, including work permits, digital nomad visas, entrepreneur visas and residence pathways for qualified applicants. 3. Spain Spain offers visas for skilled workers, entrepreneurs, students and remote workers, alongside residence options for long-term migrants. 4. Ireland Although not part of the Schengen Area, Ireland operates Critical Skills and General Employment Permit schemes that attract professionals from across the world. 5. Finland Finland has streamlined residence permits for skilled workers, researchers, students and startup founders, particularly in sectors experiencing labour shortages. 6. Sweden Sweden offers work and residence permits for qualified professionals, researchers and students, with pathways to long-term residence for eligible applicants. 7. Poland Poland has become an increasingly popular destination for international workers and students, supported by employee permits and affordable higher education. 8. Estonia Known for its digital economy, Estonia offers residence permits for skilled workers, entrepreneurs, startup founders and remote workers through its Digital Nomad Visa. 9. Czech Republic The Czech Republic provides employee cards, work permits and student residence options, making it an attractive destination for skilled professionals and international students. 10. Norway Although outside the European Union, Norway offers residence permits for skilled workers, researchers and certain self-employed professionals seeking long-term employment. Vanguard News The post 10 countries in Europe that give visas easily to Africans appeared first on Vanguard News .

Wontumi’s conviction a step in the right direction – Awula Serwah
items.description: Convener of Eco-Conscious Citizens, Awula Serwah, has welcomed the conviction and 20-year prison sentence handed to NPP Ashanti Regional Chairman Bernard Antwi Boasiako, popularly known as Chairman Wontumi, describing it as a significant milestone in Ghana’s fight against illegal mining. Speaking on JoyNews’ The Pulse on Monday, July 20, Madam Serwah said the judgment vindicates environmental campaigners, journalists and civil society groups who have consistently spoken out against the destruction of the country’s forests and water bodies. “I am pleased at the outcome. I’m pleased that Chairman Wontumi has been convicted. I’m pleased that all those who spoke out against the vandalism going on have been vindicated. I think this is a step in the right direction, and we hope that other prosecutions are going to follow,” she said. Madam Serwah argued that Chairman Wontumi is not the only influential person allegedly involved in illegal mining and called on law enforcement agencies to widen their investigations and ensure all those responsible for destroying the environment are brought to justice. She urged the authorities to sustain the momentum generated by the conviction and intensify efforts to dismantle illegal mining networks, insisting that consistent enforcement of the law is essential to protecting Ghana’s natural resources. She also commended Luv FM’s Erastus Asare Donkor for his relentless efforts in exposing illegal mining activities across the country. “First of all, I want to commend my brother, Erastus Asare Donkor, for his commitment, dedication and persistence. We want to commend him,” she said. Her comments come after the Accra High Court sentenced Chairman Wontumi to 20 years’ imprisonment over illegal mining activities on the Samreboi concession in the Western Region.

items.title: Foot : mort de l’Anglais Kevin Keegan, double Ballon d’or, à l’âge de 75 ans
items.description: L'Anglais Kevin Keegan, l'un des meilleurs footballeurs des années 1970, double Ballon d'or et vainqueur de la Ligue des champions avec Liverpool, est mort d'un cancer à l'âge de 75 ans, a annoncé lundi sa famille. Surnommé « Mighty Mouse », d'après le dessin animé « Super-Souris », en raison de sa petite taille (1,73 m) et de sa combativité, il a ensuite été un entraîneur adulé à Newcastle, avant de vivre des expériences plus mitigées, notamment sur le banc de l'équipe d'Angleterre (1999-2000). Keegan a été l'un des grands artisans de la période dorée des Reds de Liverpool, avec lesquels il remporta trois championnats d'Angleterre (1973, 1976, 1977), le premier sous les ordres de l'entraîneur légendaire Bill Shankly, qui avait eu la bonne idée de le faire passer du milieu de terrain à l'attaque. Il couronna son passage de six ans sur les bords de la Mersey par le titre européen en 1977 en battant le Borussia Mönchengladbach en finale de ce qu'on appelait alors la Coupe des clubs champions. Il laisse des souvenirs cuisants aux supporters de Saint-Étienne car, en demi-finale, il avait marqué dès la 2e minute du match retour fatal aux Verts (3-1) à Anfield Road. L'année suivante, les Reds conservèrent leur titre, mais sans Keegan, qui était parti à Hambourg dans un transfert surprise, à 26 ans, attiré par un contrat juteux à une époque où les transferts internationaux n'étaient pas aussi fréquents qu'aujourd'hui. Il brilla aussi en Allemagne, où ses performances lui valurent ses Ballons d'Or de 1978 et 1979, contribuèrent à ramener le titre de Bundesliga à Hambourg en 1979 après 19 ans de disette, mais il échoua en finale de la Coupe d'Europe des clubs champions en 1980 contre un club anglais, Nottingham Forest. Il revint alors en Angleterre, pour deux ans à Southampton et deux ans à Newcastle, où il termina sa carrière de joueur en 1984. – Fils de mineur – Ce fils de mineur, né dans la petite ville d'Armthorpe, dans le Yorkshire, en 1951, soulignait la part du travail plutôt que celle du don pur dans sa trajectoire. Il racontait volontiers avoir été snobé par plusieurs clubs avant de se faire remarquer par Liverpool. C'est assez naturellement qu'il embrassa une carrière d'entraîneur qu'il commença où il avait fini son parcours de joueur, à Newcastle. Il sauva les Magpies, englués en D2, d'une relégation, puis les fit monter en Premier League dès sa première saison complète. Les années suivantes, Newcastle lutta pour les premiers rôles, jusqu'à frôler le titre en 1996 face à Manchester United. Il quitta le club en 1997, vénéré par les supporters. Le reste de la carrière du coach Keegan fut moins marquant : des piges sans éclat à Fulham et à la tête de la sélection anglaise, qu'il quitta après un Euro 2000 raté, puis quatre saisons avec Manchester City, qu'il fit grimper en D1 mais quitta sans grande performance en 2005. Malgré un retour manqué en 2008, sa dernière tentative en tant que coach, Kevin Keegan a conservé au fil des ans une immense aura à Newcastle. « J'ai conduit le club aussi loin que je le pouvais », affirmait-il. Les supporters n'ont pas été oubliés. AFP

items.title: Peskov : les appels allemands au dialogue avec la Russie ne se traduisent pas dans les faits
items.description: Le porte-parole du Kremlin, Dmitri Peskov, a commente les informations du Times selon lesquelles l'Allemagne chercherait a retablir des canaux de communication avec la Russie. Il a confirme que Moscou restait ouverte au dialogue et n'avait jamais ete a l'origine de la rupture des relations. Il a toutefois releve que les discussions menees par les responsables europeens, notamment en Allemagne, ne s'accompagnaient pour l'instant d'aucune evolution concrete dans la politique de ces pays. Dmitri Peskov a precise que Moscou tiendrait desormais necessairement compte de l'experience acquise au cours des dernieres annees si les echanges venaient a reprendre. D'apres le Times , un changement d'etat d'esprit concernant le dialogue avec la Russie se serait produit en Allemagne. Plusieurs demarches visant a rouvrir des canaux de communication avec Moscou seraient actuellement engagees. a la fin du mois de juin, le chancelier allemand, Friedrich Merz, avait propose de geler la ligne de front en Ukraine et d'entamer des negociations avec la Russie. Il avait ensuite exprime l'espoir que Moscou accepte prochainement de participer aux discussions. Le chancelier allemand avait egalement affirme que l'OTAN, l'Union europeenne et l'Ukraine « avaient a plusieurs reprises manifeste leur volonte d'engager des negociations ». Moscou avait de son cote annonce une pause dans les negociations avec Kiev. Dmitri Peskov avait indique qu'aucun accord n'avait encore ete conclu concernant la poursuite de ce processus. Le Kremlin avait notamment pose comme condition prealable le retrait des forces ukrainiennes des nouveaux territoires russes. Le porte-parole du Kremlin avait egalement qualifie d'erreur majeure la conviction europeenne selon laquelle il serait possible de dialoguer avec la Russie depuis une position de force, en partant du principe qu'elle serait affaiblie. Selon lui, une eventuelle reprise des echanges devrait servir a rechercher des solutions et non a « imposer des lecons » a Moscou. Il a ajoute que les responsables europeens continuaient a croire en la possibilite d'infliger une « defaite strategique » a la Russie, une approche qu'il considere comme l'une des plus graves erreurs commises par l'Europe.

items.title: Osun 2026: Accord campaign council urges INEC to ensure credible governorship election
items.description: By Shina Abubakar, Osogbo OSOGBO — The Accord Party’s campaign council in Osun State has called on the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to ensure a transparent and credible conduct of the August 15, 2026 governorship election, urging the electoral body to address concerns raised from previous off-cycle elections. Speaking with journalists in Osogbo, the Director-General of the Imole Campaign Council, Senator Lere Oyewumi, said the group was raising its concerns ahead of the election to give INEC sufficient time to strengthen its preparations and reassure voters. Oyewumi said the council was concerned about reports and allegations that emerged during recent off-cycle and by-elections in some states, including claims relating to the handling of Permanent Voter Cards (PVCs), ballot papers, Bimodal Voter Accreditation System (BVAS) devices and election result sheets. He urged the electoral commission to ensure that all electoral materials and technologies deployed for the Osun governorship poll are adequately secured and protected from manipulation. According to him, the concerns expressed by the campaign council were informed by reports from election observers and public discussions surrounding previous elections. The council also called on INEC to publicly reassure residents of Osun State of its commitment to conducting a free, fair and credible election. Oyewumi further appealed to security agencies and other relevant authorities to investigate any claims capable of undermining public confidence in the electoral process and to ensure that appropriate safeguards are put in place ahead of the poll. He urged INEC to audit all BVAS devices and election result sheets to strengthen the integrity of the process and prevent any form of electoral malpractice. The campaign council maintained that transparent electoral processes are essential to sustaining public trust and ensuring that the outcome of the governorship election reflects the will of the electorate. At the time of filing this report, the Independent National Electoral Commission had not responded to the concerns raised by the campaign council. The post Osun 2026: Accord campaign council urges INEC to ensure credible governorship election appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Après le Mondial 2026, le Maroc prend le relais pour la Coupe du monde 2030
items.description: À peine la Coupe du monde 2026 achevée, le regard se tourne déjà vers le Maroc, l’Espagne et le Portugal, qui accueilleront l’édition 2030. Le Royaume prend ainsi symboliquement le relais pour un Mondial historique, celui du centenaire, qui se déroulera pour la première fois sur trois continents et dans six pays. La FIFA a officiellement confié l’organisation principale de la Coupe du monde 2030 au trio Maroc-Espagne-Portugal. Trois rencontres commémoratives seront également organisées en Uruguay, en Argentine et au Paraguay afin de célébrer les 100 ans de la première édition du tournoi, disputée en 1930 en Uruguay. Pour le Maroc, cette organisation représente l’aboutissement d’une longue ambition. Après avoir présenté plusieurs candidatures par le passé pour accueillir seul la compétition, le Royaume avait rejoint en mars 2023 le projet porté par l’Espagne et le Portugal. Le dossier tripartite était ensuite devenu l’unique candidature retenue pour l’organisation du Mondial 2030. Cette édition revêtira une dimension particulière puisqu’elle marquera le centenaire de la Coupe du monde. Pour rendre hommage à ses origines, la FIFA a prévu trois rencontres exceptionnelles en Amérique du Sud. L’Uruguay accueillera ainsi un match commémoratif à Montevideo, berceau de la première Coupe du monde remportée par la Celeste en 1930. L’Argentine, finaliste de cette première édition, accueillera également une rencontre à Buenos Aires, tandis qu’un troisième match sera organisé au Paraguay, pays où se trouve le siège de la Confédération sud-américaine de football (CONMEBOL). Après ces rencontres symboliques, l’essentiel de la compétition se déroulera au Maroc, en Espagne et au Portugal, faisant de cette édition la première Coupe du monde organisée simultanément entre l’Afrique et l’Europe. Le Maroc au cœur du dispositif Le Royaume devrait occuper une place majeure dans l’organisation avec plusieurs villes hôtes. Casablanca, Rabat, Tanger, Marrakech, Agadir et Fès figurent au cœur du dispositif marocain. Le projet phare est le Grand Stade Hassan II, dans la région de Casablanca, appelé à devenir l’une des enceintes emblématiques du Mondial 2030. Les autres grandes infrastructures footballistiques du Royaume font également l’objet d’importants travaux de construction, de rénovation et de modernisation en préparation de l’événement. L’organisation réussie de grandes compétitions internationales au cours des dernières années a par ailleurs permis au Maroc de renforcer son expérience opérationnelle avant le rendez-vous de 2030. Le Portugal accueillera pour sa part des rencontres notamment à Lisbonne et Porto, avec des enceintes majeures comme l’Estádio da Luz, l’Estádio José Alvalade et l’Estádio do Dragão. L’Espagne disposera du plus grand nombre de stades candidats, avec plusieurs enceintes emblématiques réparties dans différentes villes du pays. Une édition exceptionnelle pour le centenaire La Coupe du monde 2030 devrait se dérouler du 8 juin au 21 juillet, sur une période particulièrement longue destinée notamment à tenir compte du format élargi de la compétition et de sa dimension intercontinentale. Cette configuration fera du Mondial 2030 une édition sans précédent : six pays, trois continents et un siècle d’histoire réunis autour d’une même compétition. La question du stade qui accueillera la finale demeure toutefois l’un des principaux enjeux à trancher. Plusieurs grandes enceintes figurent parmi les options évoquées, alors que le choix définitif doit revenir à la FIFA. Après les États-Unis, le Canada et le Mexique en 2026, le Maroc, l’Espagne et le Portugal prennent désormais le relais avec l’objectif d’organiser une Coupe du monde à la hauteur de son centenaire. Pour le Royaume, 2030 constituera surtout un rendez-vous historique : celui d’accueillir pour la première fois sur son sol des rencontres de la plus prestigieuse compétition de football au monde. The post Après le Mondial 2026, le Maroc prend le relais pour la Coupe du monde 2030 appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .

items.title: Oil transfer activity in Gulf of Oman slows following ship attacks, data shows
items.description: By Jonathan Saul LONDON, July 20 (Reuters) - Oil transfers between tankers in waters outside the Strait of Hormuz have slowed following a wave of recent attacks on vessels by Iranian forces, according to satellite analysis and sources. Since the start of the Iran war in late February, ship-to-ship (STS) transfers between tankers have helped enable oil to leave the Gulf despite the threats to commercial vessels sailing through the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Yemen's Houthis declare naval blockade against Saudi Arabia
items.description: By Nayera Abdallah and Timour Azhari DUBAI, July 20 (Reuters) - Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis said on Monday they were imposing a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, a move that threatens to escalate the conflict in the region and disrupt global energy supply and trade beyond the Gulf. The Houthis' armed forces in a statement said they were declaring "a maritime embargo against the criminal Saudi enemy, based on the equation of 'an eye for an eye', effective immediately".

items.title: Iru Ekun security network enrolment surpasses 80,000 – Igboho
items.description: By Adeola Badru Chief Sunday Adeyemo, popularly known as Sunday Igboho, has disclosed that the number of volunteers registered under the Iru Ekun Security Network has exceeded 80,000 across the South-West, describing the milestone as a clear demonstration of the region’s commitment to tackling insecurity. Igboho, the founder of the security outfit, said the overwhelming response reflects the patriotism, confidence and determination of Yoruba people to support community-based efforts aimed at combating kidnapping, terrorism and other violent crimes. In a statement issued by his media office on Monday, Igboho stated that since the launch of the Iru Ekun Security Network, communities across the South-West that were previously plagued by criminal activities have witnessed a significant decline in kidnapping incidents. He stressed that comprehensive background checks are being carried out on all prospective members to prevent individuals with questionable character or criminal records from infiltrating the organisation. “It is delightful to announce the impressive level of interest and enthusiasm demonstrated by our people in the Iru Ekun Security Network. We projected a strength of 50,000 personnel, but we have already registered over 80,000 volunteers across Yorubaland.“ “The security outfit has been officially launched in Oyo and Osun states. We have also concluded plans to launch it in Kwara State on Thursday. Critical stakeholders, including Governor AbdulRahman AbdulRazaq, are partnering with us in the state.“ “As we continue to lawfully combat crime, particularly kidnapping and other terrorist activities capable of threatening the peace and safety of our rural communities, Nigerians should continue to support President Bola Ahmed Tinubu’s initiative to strengthen collaboration between the federal government and local security structures in addressing insecurity.” Igboho further stated that cases of abduction had reduced drastically across the South-West, attributing the improvement to effective intelligence gathering by local communities and sustained collaboration with federal security agencies. According to him, the partnership involving the Nigeria Police Force, the Ministry of Defence, the Armed Forces, the Office of the National Security Adviser (ONSA) and the Department of State Services (DSS) has strengthened public confidence in the Federal Government’s ability to safeguard lives and property. “The Chief of Defence Staff, General Christopher Musa; the Inspector-General of Police, Kayode Egbetokun; Deputy Inspector-General of Police, Tunji Disu; the Director-General of the DSS, Adeola Oluwatosin Ajayi; and the National Security Adviser, Nuhu Ribadu, as well as Commissioners of Police in the South-West, Kwara and Niger States, have greatly supported our operations. Without their backing, the successes we have recorded would not have been possible.” He also commended the governors of the South-West for their continued support of the Iru Ekun Security Network and urged Nigerians, irrespective of ethnicity, tribe or religion, to regard security as a shared responsibility requiring the collective commitment of every citizen. The post Iru Ekun security network enrolment surpasses 80,000 – Igboho appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: England, Liverpool and Newcastle legend Kevin Keegan dies aged 75
items.description: Kevin Keegan has died at the age of 75. It was announced in January that the former England player and manager had been diagnosed with stage four cancer, with his family revealing he was set to begin treatment. “It is with immense sadness that we announce that Kevin Keegan has passed away at the age of 75,” a statement from Keegan’s family read. “The former England player and manager had been battling cancer and was surrounded by his wife and daughters in his final moments. “Kevin, a double Ballon D’or winner, was a much loved husband, father and grandfather. The family would like to thank Kevin’s incredible medical team for all their support. This is a hugely difficult time and they are requesting space and privacy.” Image:It was announced in January Kevin Keegan had been diagnosed with stage four cancer Keegan was considered one of the best players of his generation and enjoyed a stellar playing career, making almost 750 club appearances, lifting the First Division trophy four times with Liverpool and winning the Ballon d’Or back-to-back in 1978 and 1979 while playing for Hamburg in the Bundesliga. Image:Liverpool’s Ian Callaghan, Kevin Keegan and Ray Clemence parade the FA Cup Image:Liverpool striker Kevin Keegan during a match with QPR Seven years after his retirement, he took his first coaching role as manager of a struggling Newcastle team in February 1992. The Magpies were in danger of being relegated to Division Two but Keegan kept them up and then led them to the Premier League in his first full season in charge. Then, in 1996, he took Newcastle to within touching distance of their first top-tier title since 1927. Newcastle were Premier League title favourites for most of that 1995/96 season, at one stage being 12 points clear of Manchester United. But a dramatic collapse from Keegan’s side coupled with a Sir Alex Ferguson-inspired Man Utd winning 13 of their last 15, eventually saw Newcastle finish second by four points. Late defeats at the hands of Liverpool and Nottingham Forest in the run-in saw United eventually haul them in. As the pressure mounted on a beleaguered Keegan, who was desperate to deliver the title to a passionate Newcastle fanbase, his infamous “I’d love it..” post-match interview after victory at Leeds, aimed at Sir Alex, among others, unfortunately characterised his time at St James’ Park. He would go on to manage England, for whom he had scored 21 goals in 63 games as a player, between February 1999 and October 2000 – resigning minutes after a 1-0 defeat to Germany in the last game played at the old Wembley Stadium. Image:Kevin Keegan celebrates scoring against Scotland at Wembley in 1979 His time as national boss also saw England fail to get out of the group stage at Euro 2000, despite a 1-0 win over Germany, as defeats by Portugal and Romania piled on the pressure even before that defeat at the hands of Dietmar Hamann’s free-kick four months later in the first qualifier for the 2002 World Cup. A spell at Manchester City followed, which included promotion to the Premier League in 2002 as Division One champions, but Keegan left the club in March 2005, initially announcing his retirement from football. Keegan made an emotional return to Newcastle in 2008, but only lasted eight months following tensions with the board and owner Mike Ashley.

items.title: São Tomé and Príncipe President Vila Nova wins second term, preliminary results show
items.description: São Tomé and Príncipe President Carlos Vila Nova has won a second ​five-year term, according to preliminary results from the election commission ‌announced on Monday. Vila Nova, a former public works and infrastructure minister, won Sunday’s election with more than 55% of the vote, enough to avoid ​a runoff, the commission said during a press conference. His ​top challenger, Nito Abreu, received more than 41% of ⁠the vote. Vila Nova came to power in 2021 with the ​backing of the Independent Democratic Action party (ADI), which holds a majority ​of seats in the National Assembly. But last year Vila Nova dismissed Patrice Trovoada as prime minister, prompting a split with the ADI that left him ​running as an independent on Sunday against three other candidates. Abreu ​had vowed to focus on creating jobs for youth and stemming what he ‌describes as an exodus of young people from the country, which is made up of two main islands and has a population of around 240,000. A former Portuguese colony situated off the western ​coast of Africa, ​São Tomé and ⁠Principe has had a history of free and peaceful elections since the adoption of multiparty politics ​in 1990. The country produces cocoa and was once ​seen as ⁠a potential oil producer, though it has not yielded major finds. In 2022, the government announced it had thwarted a coup attempt, the ⁠first in nearly two decades, involving at ​least one member of a South African apartheid-era militia known as the Buffalo Battalion.

items.title: Wontumi’s 20-year jail term shocking; NPP must appeal – NPP communicator
items.description: An aspiring Ashanti Regional Communications Officer of the New Patriotic Party (NPP), Ishaq Kyei-Brobbey, says the conviction and 20-year prison sentence handed to the party’s Ashanti Regional Chairman, Bernard Antwi Boasiako, popularly known as Chairman Wontumi, has come as a shock to him. Speaking on JoyNews after Wontumi’s conviction, Mr Kyei-Brobbey described the court’s decision as saddening, noting that Wontumi remains the party’s Ashanti Regional Chairman and has created opportunities for many party members, including himself. “The news has come as a shock to me. It’s a moment of mixed feelings because he is still the Ashanti Regional Chairman of the New Patriotic Party. He has not resigned, and he remains a leader of the party in the region,” he said. Mr Kyei-Brobbey said although he is not a legal practitioner, he believes the party should immediately pursue an appeal against the sentence. “I am not a lawyer, so it is difficult for me to make legal arguments, but I believe there is an opportunity for an appeal, and I hope it will be pursued expeditiously,” he stated. He expressed hope that the appellate process could result in Wontumi’s release or a reduction in his sentence, citing the founder of Heaven Way Champions International Ministry, Patricia Asiedua, popularly known as Nana Agradaa’s case, where convictions were reviewed on appeal. According to him, the party’s legal team should urgently assess the judgment and determine the next course of action. “I’m saddened by the outcome of the court case against Chairman Wontumi, and I’m praying that the legal brains and legal luminaries of the party and beyond will sit immediately and consider the next option,” he said. Mr Kyei-Brobbey added that while he was pleased with his own progress in the race for the Ashanti Regional Communications Officer position, the news of Wontumi’s conviction overshadowed the moment. He expressed optimism that the appeal process would be handled swiftly and could ultimately secure Wontumi’s freedom or a reduction in his sentence. His comments follow the Accra High Court’s decision to sentence Chairman Wontumi to 20 years’ imprisonment over illegal mining activities on the Samreboi concession in the Western Region. Justice Audrey Kocuvie-Tay, who presided over the Criminal Division of the High Court, delivered the sentence shortly after 11:40 am on Monday, July 20, 2026, bringing to an end one of Ghana’s most closely watched illegal mining prosecutions. The court also imposed a fine of 10,000 penalty units, equivalent to GH¢120,000, with a default sentence of three years’ imprisonment if the fine is not paid.

items.title: Zondoma : Une mosquée de vendredi inaugurée à Rassomdé dans la commune de Tougo avec des prières pour la paix
items.description: Burkina/Zondoma-Inauguration-Mosquée Zondoma : Une mosquée de vendredi inaugurée à Rassomdé dans la commune de Tougo avec des prières pour la paix Tougo, 17 juillet 2026 (AIB)-Les fidèles musulmans de Rotto, dans le village de Rassomdé, commune de Tougo dans la province du Zondoma, ont inauguré, le vendredi 17 juillet 2026, une mosquée de vendredi. La cérémonie a été marquée par des prières pour le retour de la paix au Burkina Faso et des bénédictions à l’endroit des Forces combattantes. La nouvelle infrastructure religieuse devra désormais servir de cadre de rassemblement des fidèles musulmans pour les prières du vendredi, mais aussi de lieu de promotion de la paix, de la cohésion sociale et du vivre-ensemble. Dans son sermon, l’imam El Hadj Mahamadi Traoré a invité les fidèles à faire de cette mosquée un espace de communion et d’entente entre les membres de la communauté. Il les a également exhortés à multiplier les prières en faveur de la paix et à soutenir moralement les forces engagées dans la lutte contre l’insécurité. « Nous devons à tout moment et en tout lieu prier pour le retour de la paix et encourager nos FDS et VDP, car c’est eux qui nous permettent de pratiquer notre religion dans la quiétude », a déclaré l’imam Mahamadi Traoré. Pour le président du comité de gestion de la mosquée, Soumaïla Romba, la réalisation de cet édifice constitue une grande satisfaction pour les populations de la localité. « Cette infrastructure est une fierté pour notre village. Elle est le fruit de la contribution des fils et filles de la localité qui se sont mobilisés pour sa réalisation. Nous souhaitons qu’elle soit un lieu où les fidèles se retrouvent dans la paix et la fraternité », a-t-il indiqué. Selon lui, la construction de la mosquée traduit également l’engagement de la communauté à renforcer les valeurs religieuses et sociales dans le village de Rassomdé. La cérémonie d’inauguration a connu la participation des fidèles musulmans, des responsables religieux et des personnes ressources de la localité, venus témoigner leur reconnaissance et formuler des prières pour la paix et la stabilité du pays. Agence d’information du Burkina YN/AK-DNK

items.title: Air Algérie : le nouveau Boeing 737 Max entre en service
items.description: Air Algérie a commandé dix Boeing 737 MAX 8 et a réceptionné le premier appareil il y a une semaine. L’appareil flambant neuf est utilisé sur les vols nationaux et internationaux court et moyen-courriers. Il a déjà entamé sa carrière. Le nouvel appareil a effectué son premier vol le 13 juillet entre Alger et Tunis. Il a ensuite assuré une liaison entre Alger et Londres-Heathrow le 14 juillet 2026. Selon le site aeroroutes, le Boeing 737 Max assurera cinq vols hebdomadaires sur la ligne d’ Air Algérie entre Alger et Londres Heathrow en août prochain, ainsi que d’autres liaisons qui n’ont pas été précisées. Ces avions de nouvelle génération permettent à la compagnie aérienne nationale de moderniser sa flotte et d’offrir de meilleures conditions de voyage à ses passagers. Grâce à cette acquisition, Air Algérie pourra également ouvrir de nouvelles lignes, renforcer certaines dessertes déjà assurées et réduire le recours à l’affrètement pour faire à la demande notamment en été. Air Algérie acquiert 28 nouveaux avions : 18 Boeing et 10 Airbus Cette acquisition s'inscrit dans le cadre du programme de modernisation de la flotte d'Air Algérie. Grâce à ses meilleures performances et à une consommation de carburant réduite, le Boeing 737 MAX 8 permettra à la compagnie de gagner en efficacité. Dans le cadre du renouvellement de sa flotte, Air Algérie a également commandé dix Airbus A330-900Neo, dont cinq ont déjà été réceptionnés, huit Boeing 737 MAX 9, dix Boeing 737 MAX 8, dont le premier vient d’être livré, ainsi que 16 ATR 72-600 destinés à sa filiale Domestic Airlines. Un premier ATR a été livré il y a une semaine L’article Air Algérie : le nouveau Boeing 737 Max entre en service est apparu en premier sur TSA .

items.title: Le Sénégal décroche le premier prix du jury au Festival algéro-africain du théâtre universitaire
items.description: Le Sénégal s'est illustré sur la scène culturelle africaine en remportant le Premier Prix du Jury au Festival algéro-africain du théâtre universitaire, organisé à Alger (Algérie). Cette rencontre a réuni des écoles supérieures d'art dramatique de 16 pays africains, venues des quatre coins du continent pour célébrer la création théâtrale universitaire. La distinction a été décernée à la pièce « Graine de la discorde », une œuvre qui porte un puissant message sur l'unité africaine et les conséquences destructrices de la vengeance. La pièce raconte l'histoire de Mamadou et Demba, deux amis inséparables. Leur relation bascule le jour où la poule rousse de Mamadou s'introduit dans le champ de Demba et picore une simple graine. En représailles, Demba tue la poule. Blessé et animé par la colère, Mamadou met le feu au champ de son ancien ami. L'incendie se propage rapidement et ravage une grande partie du village. Ce qui n'était au départ qu'un banal différend dégénère progressivement en un conflit de grande ampleur, jusqu'à prendre les proportions d'une guerre mondiale. À travers cette mise en scène, les auteurs délivrent une leçon universelle : il n'existe pas de petites querelles, mais seulement de petits esprits qui ignorent jusqu'où peut mener la vengeance. Un appel fort au dialogue, à la tolérance et à la préservation de l'unité entre les peuples africains. Le Festival algéro-africain du théâtre universitaire, organisé du 13 au 20 juillet à Alger, a également décerné des prix dans les catégories de la meilleure composition musicale, de la meilleure scénographie, du meilleur texte, du meilleur comédien, de la meilleure comédienne et du meilleur spectacle. Le Premier Prix du Jury remporté par le Sénégal vient consacrer le talent de la jeune création théâtrale sénégalaise et son rayonnement sur la scène culturelle africaine.

items.title: The World Cup Could Not Be Americanized
items.description: A World Cup on North American soil ended with the most thoroughly imported, overseas-style final match possible. The foreign tourists who tried to smuggle ranch dressing through TSA; the Norwegians who scarfed down Texas brisket while doing the Viking row at Dallas barbecue joints; the garish, out-of-place halftime show in a New Jersey football stadium—all of that Americanness was overcome in the end by the classic continental display of footwork and defense in Spain’s 1–0 victory over Argentina. This event just could not be Americanized, hard as Donald Trump, the Fox network, and the Muppets tried. Men’s soccer did not become American at this World Cup—and America still does not really play men’s soccer. Real soccer looked like this: one tap, two taps, and then flights of red Spanish jerseys surging toward the goal. La Roja played such elegant, staccato, soft-shoed ball that they seemed to have 20 men on the field instead of 11. With their quick-step precision, they completed a dazzling 789 passes. Sorry, but Americans just don’t kick it like that—not yet, anyway. Fully 89 percent of Spain’s touches, whether nosing the ball along or lashing it, went right where they intended. But the game, almost incomprehensibly, also looked like this: Despite Spain’s brilliance, it was a scoreless tie right up until the 105th minute of extra time, when Ferran Torres laced a goal with a left-footer to the high back net. All of that nullity was thanks to the record-breaking heroics of Argentina’s goalie Damian Emiliano “Dibu” Martínez, who had 11 saves, the most recorded in a final since the World Cup began keeping stats. He launched himself sideways, again and again, to get his gloves on Spain’s ballistic leg whips. Spain completely dominated—they took 20 shots overall compared with just two for Argentina—and yet they could have lost. That’s “the football” for you. It’s a game of low scores, which can mean rough justice. A team can be clearly superior and not get a bit of what it deserves. As Argentina’s captain, Lionel Messi, reflected a couple of years ago in a TV interview , “In football you can be 10 times better than your opponent and still lose.” He added, “These things in football are difficult to explain no matter how much you analyze and study it.” [ Read: America’s greatest food export ] This game was likely the final World Cup appearance for Messi, the greatest player of his generation if not all time, whom Spain limited to just one shot, which was blocked. Argentina had trouble even getting the ball in Spain’s half of the field, unable to make connections against a quicker-toed, fast-closing defense that gave up just a single goal in its eight World Cup matches. Yet Messi still managed to create a sense of late drama with his hooking passes across the face of Spain’s net, conjuring sudden opportunities out of nothing. American players just don’t seem to have that kind of threat in their shoes. Think about the difference between American soccer prodigies and a Messi, who took up the game at the age of 3 or 4 in his sprawling home city of Rosario. The son of a house cleaner and a supervisor in a metal factory, he played for a local nonprofit club that sometimes didn’t have enough practice balls to go around. His footwork is partly a function of improvised games he played by himself as a boy; as he related in an early TV interview , he would juggle oranges and tennis balls off his insteps. He could kick an orange 60 times in a row without it hitting the ground; a tennis ball, 120 times. One of Messi’s earliest coaches, Carlo Marconi, once recalled that he baited Messi with cookies for every goal he scored. Messi had a particular passion for alfajores , caramel-flavored treats, and would score four or five goals a game to get them. He scored so often that Marconi decided to change the game: He would give Messi a double reward if he could score on a header. Marconi recalled: “So what did he do? He would start here, dribble past everyone, get to the goal, kick the ball up with his foot, and score with his head. Then he’d say to me, ‘Two.’” But Marconi also remembered that Messi would divide the spoils among his teammates, handing out alfajores to other boys. Marconi said he ended up just giving Messi cookies for free: “Here, take it. But I’m giving it to you not because you’re a good player, but because you’re a good person.” That story almost certainly wouldn’t happen today in America, for the simple reason that the youth-soccer culture here is mainly built on a profit-driven “pay to play” system. Parents fork over heavy fees to enroll their child in local clubs that charge for training, field rentals, coaching, and travel. As a result, soccer remains a mostly suburban, middle- and upper-class sport. (Not that soccer is the only game that requires American parents to dig in their pockets: According to one study , the cost of all youth sports rose by 46 percent from 2019 to 2025.) [ Read: Soccer’s most magical performer ] The cure for this problem is not more spending. The U.S. Soccer Federation spent more than $6 million in yearly salary and incentives on an illustrious European head coach, Mauricio Pochettino, yet his overhyped team was beaten, badly, the minute it faced a top-10-ranked team. Belgium’s 4–1victory over the U.S. in the round of 16 came despite intercession by the American president to have the suspension of the U.S.’s best player, Folarin Balogun, overturned after he was given a red card. “Overturn this,” the Belgium team posted on its social-media account after the game. Balogun, by the way, is really the product of British—not American—soccer. He has U.S. birthright citizenship because he was born in Brooklyn to a Nigerian mother who was merely visiting. He was reared and trained in London. Trump’s heavy shoulder lean on FIFA regarding the red card—“I was the one who got them to do it,” he acknowledged—soured much of the goodwill that foreign fans may have had toward the U.S. as a co-host of the tournament. Unfortunately, another breach of FIFA rules and tradition came at halftime of the final and didn’t win many friends either. FIFA mandates that halftime should be no longer than 15 minutes. Soccer—real football—is a game of rhythm and fluidity, with only a brief break. There had never been a “halftime show” at a World Cup. Yet because this World Cup was in America, a halftime show there was. The verdict on the 27-minute festival of gaudiness, featuring Madonna, Justin Bieber, Shakira, and various Muppet characters, is that America showed off better when it didn’t try quite so hard. It was a relief when Spain retook the field, restoring a sense of elegance, and leaving a far more lingering imprint on the field.
